It is a [[Gokoku Shrines|Gokoku Shrine]], or a shrine dedicated to war dead.{{Efn|Is this [[WP:BLUE]] considering that the shrine is literally named as such?}} Such shrines were made to serve to enshrine the war dead, and they were all considered "branches" of [[Yasukuni Shrine]]. They were renamed from [[Shōkonsha|Shokonsha]] in 1939.{{Cite journal |last=TAKAYAMA |first=K. PETER |date=1990 |title=Enshrinement and Persistency of Japanese Religion |url=https://www.jstor.org/stable/23917081 |journal=Journal of Church and State |volume=32 |issue=3 |pages=527–547 |issn=0021-969X}} The Okinawa Gokoku-jinja Shrine also enshrines the dead of the [[Battle of Okinawa]], including ordinary residents, schoolchildren in distress, and civilian war dead.[[護国神社#山中|山中 2013]]、212頁{{Cite web |date=2022-03-08 |title=沖縄県護国神社について｜初詣、ご祈願、地鎮祭は、沖縄県護国神社 |url=https://www.okinawa-gokoku.jp/about.php |url-status=dead |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20220308190702/https://www.okinawa-gokoku.jp/about.php |archive-date=8 March 2022 |access-date=2022-03-08 |website=}} In addition, about 10,000 mobilized students and female volunteer corps members who were victims of the [[Atomic bomb]] are also enshrined as deities at [[Hiroshima Gokoku Shrine]].[[護国神社#山中|山中 2013]]、154頁{{Cite web |date=2021-01-20 |title=由緒 {{!}} 広島護國神社 |url=https://www.h-gokoku.or.jp/yuisho/ |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20210120233005/https://www.h-gokoku.or.jp/yuisho/ |archive-date=2021-01-20 |access-date=2022-03-08}} The main hall burned down during the [[Pacific War]] and was reconstructed in 1959.{{Cite book |last=Takenaka |first=Akiko |url=http://dx.doi.org/10.1515/9780824856939 |title=Yasukuni Shrine |date=2017-10-16 |publisher=University of Hawaii Press |isbn=978-0-8248-5693-9 |pages=158}} == Enshrined Deities == The shrine enshrines spirits of war dead from Okinawa Prefecture and mainland Japan, including civilians and student casualties who perished during the {{ill|Battle of Okinawa|ja|沖縄戦}}. As of October 2005, the shrine enshrines 178,689 spirits, the highest number among {{ill|Gokoku shrines|ja|護国神社}} nationwide. == History == [[File:Okinawaken_Gogoku_Shrine_torii.jpeg|thumb|right|170px|Torii gate]] The shrine traces its origins to a {{ill|Shōkonsha|ja|招魂社}} established in 1936 to honor those who died in service to Japan during conflicts such as the {{ill|First Sino-Japanese War|ja|日清戦争}} and the {{ill|Russo-Japanese War|ja|日露戦争}}.加治順人『沖縄の神社』p115　ひるぎ社、2000年10月30日 In 1940, it was designated as a Gokoku Shrine by the Ministry of Home Affairs and renamed "Okinawa Prefecture Gokoku Shrine."『全國護國神社會五十年史』（1997年3月1日、全國護國神社會発行）287頁。 The shrine was destroyed during the {{ill|Battle of Okinawa|ja|沖縄戦}} in April 1945. A temporary shrine was constructed on April 26, 1959. In the same year, relics of spirits from the {{ill|Yasukuni Shrine|ja|靖国神社}} were transferred to the shrine, honoring mainland Japanese who died in the Battle of Okinawa. The current main shrine building was completed in October 1965, with the relocation ceremony held on November 19 and the restoration commemorative festival on November 20.加治順人『沖縄の神社』p143　ひるぎ社、2000年10月30日 In 1980, a Kaguraden (dance hall) was added to the grounds.{{DEFAULTSORT:Okinawa Prefecture Gokoku Shrine}} [[Category:Shinto shrines in Okinawa Prefecture]] [[Category:Naha]] [[Category:Gokoku shrines]] [[Category:Independent Shinto shrines]] [[Category:Former prefectural shrines]] [[Category:Buildings and structures in Naha]] [[Category:History of Okinawa Prefecture]] == See Also == * [[Controversies surrounding Yasukuni Shrine]] * [[Martyrs' shrines (China)|Martyr's Shrines]] * [[:en:Martial temple|Martial temple]] and [[:en:Wen Wu temple|Wen Wu temple]] * [[National Revolutionary Martyrs' Shrine]] * [[Eternal Spring Shrine]] * [[:en:Chinese Cultural Renaissance|Chinese Cultural Renaissance]] * [[Ancestral shrine]] * [[:en:Gallant Garden|Gallant Garden]] * [[Gokoku Shrines]] * [[:en:Tomb of the Unknown Soldier|Tomb of the Unknown Soldier]] * [[:en:Arlington National Cemetery|Arlington National Cemetery]] * [[:en:Valhalla|Valhalla]] (home to the souls of fallen warriors in Scandinavian mythology) * [[:en:Walhalla (memorial)|Walhalla Shrine]] (a hall of fame in Germany honoring "commendable and honorable Germans") * [[Eternal Spring Shrine]] * [[The common end of myriad good deeds]] * [[:en:Greek hero cult|Greek hero cult]] == Notes == {{Notelist}} == References == {{Reflist}} == External Links == * [http://www.okinawa-gokoku.jp/ Official website of Okinawa Prefecture Gokoku Shrine] {{Shinto Horizontal}} {{Shinto-stub}} {{DEFAULTSORT:Okinawa Prefecture Gokoku Shrine}} [[Category:Shinto shrines in Okinawa Prefecture]] [[Category:Naha]] [[Category:Gokoku shrines]] [[Category:Independent Shinto shrines]] [[Category:Former prefectural shrines]] [[Category:Buildings and structures in Naha]] [[Category:History of Okinawa Prefecture]] {{Draft categories| [[Category:Gokoku shrines]] [[Category:1875 establishments]] [[Category:Shinto shrines in Okinawa Prefecture]] [[Category:original pages]]{{Gokoku shrines}} [[Category:Gokoku shrines]] [[Category:dd]] [[Category:bump]] [[ja:沖縄県護国神社]] }} {{⚠️🚨COPIED from EverybodyWiki ❗❕⚠️😡😤Please respect Licence CC-BY-SA ❗}} {{Source Wikipedia}}

They were renamed from [[Shōkonsha|Shokonsha]] in 1939.{{Cite journal |last=TAKAYAMA |first=K. PETER |date=1990 |title=Enshrinement and Persistency of Japanese Religion |url=https://www.jstor.org/stable/23917081 |journal=Journal of Church and State |volume=32 |issue=3 |pages=527–547 |issn=0021-969X}} The Okinawa Gokoku-jinja Shrine also enshrines the dead of the [[Battle of Okinawa]], including ordinary residents, schoolchildren in distress, and civilian war dead.[[護国神社#山中|山中 2013]]、212頁{{Cite web |date=2022-03-08 |title=沖縄県護国神社について｜初詣、ご祈願、地鎮祭は、沖縄県護国神社 |url=https://www.okinawa-gokoku.jp/about.php |url-status=dead |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20220308190702/https://www.okinawa-gokoku.jp/about.php |archive-date=8 March 2022 |access-date=2022-03-08 |website=}} In addition, about 10,000 mobilized students and female volunteer corps members who were victims of the [[Atomic bomb]] are also enshrined as deities at [[Hiroshima Gokoku Shrine]].[[護国神社#山中|山中 2013]]、154頁{{Cite web |date=2021-01-20 |title=由緒 {{!}} 広島護國神社 |url=https://www.h-gokoku.or.jp/yuisho/ |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20210120233005/https://www.h-gokoku.or.jp/yuisho/ |archive-date=2021-01-20 |access-date=2022-03-08}} The main hall burned down during the [[Pacific War]] and was reconstructed in 1959.{{Cite book |last=Takenaka |first=Akiko |url=http://dx.doi.org/10.1515/9780824856939 |title=Yasukuni Shrine |date=2017-10-16 |publisher=University of Hawaii Press |isbn=978-0-8248-5693-9 |pages=158}} == Enshrined Deities == The shrine enshrines spirits of war dead from Okinawa Prefecture and mainland Japan, including civilians and student casualties who perished during the {{ill|Battle of Okinawa|ja|沖縄戦}}. As of October 2005, the shrine enshrines 178,689 spirits, the highest number among {{ill|Gokoku shrines|ja|護国神社}} nationwide. == History == [[File:Okinawaken_Gogoku_Shrine_torii.jpeg|thumb|right|170px|Torii gate]] The shrine traces its origins to a {{ill|Shōkonsha|ja|招魂社}} established in 1936 to honor those who died in service to Japan during conflicts such as the {{ill|First Sino-Japanese War|ja|日清戦争}} and the {{ill|Russo-Japanese War|ja|日露戦争}}.加治順人『沖縄の神社』p115　ひるぎ社、2000年10月30日 In 1940, it was designated as a Gokoku Shrine by the Ministry of Home Affairs and renamed "Okinawa Prefecture Gokoku Shrine."『全國護國神社會五十年史』（1997年3月1日、全國護國神社會発行）287頁。 The shrine was destroyed during the {{ill|Battle of Okinawa|ja|沖縄戦}} in April 1945. A temporary shrine was constructed on April 26, 1959. In the same year, relics of spirits from the {{ill|Yasukuni Shrine|ja|靖国神社}} were transferred to the shrine, honoring mainland Japanese who died in the Battle of Okinawa. The current main shrine building was completed in October 1965, with the relocation ceremony held on November 19 and the restoration commemorative festival on November 20.加治順人『沖縄の神社』p143　ひるぎ社、2000年10月30日 In 1980, a Kaguraden (dance hall) was added to the grounds.{{DEFAULTSORT:Okinawa Prefecture Gokoku Shrine}} [[Category:Shinto shrines in Okinawa Prefecture]] [[Category:Naha]] [[Category:Gokoku shrines]] [[Category:Independent Shinto shrines]] [[Category:Ken-sha]] [[Category:Buildings and structures in Naha]] [[Category:History of Okinawa Prefecture]] == See Also == * [[Controversies surrounding Yasukuni Shrine]] * [[Martyrs' shrines (China)|Martyr's Shrines]] * [[:en:Martial temple|Martial temple]] and [[:en:Wen Wu temple|Wen Wu temple]] * [[National Revolutionary Martyrs' Shrine]] * [[Eternal Spring Shrine]] * [[:en:Chinese Cultural Renaissance|Chinese Cultural Renaissance]] * [[Ancestral shrine]] * [[:en:Gallant Garden|Gallant Garden]] * [[Gokoku Shrines]] * [[:en:Tomb of the Unknown Soldier|Tomb of the Unknown Soldier]] * [[:en:Arlington National Cemetery|Arlington National Cemetery]] * [[:en:Valhalla|Valhalla]] (home to the souls of fallen warriors in Scandinavian mythology) * [[:en:Walhalla (memorial)|Walhalla Shrine]] (a hall of fame in Germany honoring "commendable and honorable Germans") * [[Eternal Spring Shrine]] * [[The common end of myriad good deeds]] * [[:en:Greek hero cult|Greek hero cult]] == Notes == {{Notelist}} == References == {{Reflist}} == External Links == * [http://www.okinawa-gokoku.jp/ Official website of Okinawa Prefecture Gokoku Shrine] {{Shinto Horizontal}} {{Shinto-stub}} {{DEFAULTSORT:Okinawa Prefecture Gokoku Shrine}} [[Category:Shinto shrines in Okinawa Prefecture]] [[Category:Naha]] (Formerly ''[[Shokonsha]]'') | leadership = | website = | architecture_style = | founded_by = | established = | date_destroyed = | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | name = Okinawa Prefecture Gokoku Shrine | image = [[File:Okinawaken Gokoku Shrine haiden.jpg|250px|Okinawa Prefecture Gokoku Shrine Haiden]]
Haiden | coordinates = {{Coord|26|12|11.6|N|127|40|33.6|E|scale:25000_region:JP-47_type:landmark|display=inline,title}} | location = 奥武山町44, Naha City, Okinawa Prefecture, Japan | deity = * Local war dead enshrined at {{ill|Yasukuni Shrine|ja|靖国神社}} * War dead from mainland Japan who perished during the {{ill|Battle of Okinawa|ja|沖縄戦}} * Civilian victims and student casualties of the war | established = 1936 (Shōwa 11) | festivals = April 23, October 23 }} '''Okinawa Prefecture Gokoku Shrine''' ({{nihongo|沖縄県護国神社|Okinawaken Gokoku Jinja}}) is a [[Shinto shrine]] located within {{ill|Onoyama Park|ja|奥武山公園}}, {{ill|Naha|ja|那覇市}}, Okinawa Prefecture, Japan. The shrine is an independent religious institution not affiliated with [[Jinja Honcho]]. {{Interlanguage link|{{PAGENAME}}|ja|沖縄県護国神社}} is a [[Shinto shrine]] located in [[Japan]]. It is a [[Gokoku Shrines|Gokoku Shrine]], or a shrine dedicated to war dead.{{Efn|Is this [[WP:BLUE]] considering that the shrine is literally named as such?}} Such shrines were made to serve to enshrine the war dead, and they were all considered "branches" of [[Yasukuni Shrine]]. They were renamed from [[Shōkonsha|Shokonsha]] in 1939.{{Cite journal |last=TAKAYAMA |first=K. As of October 2005, the shrine enshrines 178,689 spirits, the highest number among {{ill|Gokoku shrines|ja|護国神社}} nationwide. == History == [[File:Okinawaken_Gogoku_Shrine_torii.jpeg|thumb|right|170px|Torii gate]] The shrine traces its origins to a {{ill|Shōkonsha|ja|招魂社}} established in 1936 to honor those who died in service to Japan during conflicts such as the {{ill|First Sino-Japanese War|ja|日清戦争}} and the {{ill|Russo-Japanese War|ja|日露戦争}}.加治順人『沖縄の神社』p115　ひるぎ社、2000年10月30日 In 1940, it was designated as a Gokoku Shrine by the Ministry of Home Affairs and renamed "Okinawa Prefecture Gokoku Shrine."『全國護國神社會五十年史』（1997年3月1日、全國護國神社會発行）287頁。 The shrine was destroyed during the {{ill|Battle of Okinawa|ja|沖縄戦}} in April 1945. A temporary shrine was constructed on April 26, 1959. In the same year, relics of spirits from the {{ill|Yasukuni Shrine|ja|靖国神社}} were transferred to the shrine, honoring mainland Japanese who died in the Battle of Okinawa. The current main shrine building was completed in October 1965, with the relocation ceremony held on November 19 and the restoration commemorative festival on November 20.加治順人『沖縄の神社』p143　ひるぎ社、2000年10月30日 In 1980, a Kaguraden (dance hall) was added to the grounds.{{DEFAULTSORT:Okinawa Prefecture Gokoku Shrine}} [[Category:Shinto shrines in Okinawa Prefecture]] [[Category:Naha]] [[Category:Gokoku shrines]] [[Category:Independent Shinto shrines]] [[Category:Ken-sha]] [[Category:Buildings and structures in Naha]] [[Category:History of Okinawa Prefecture]] == See Also == * [[Controversies surrounding Yasukuni Shrine]] * [[Martyrs' shrines (China)|Martyr's Shrines]] * [[:en:Martial temple|Martial temple]] and [[:en:Wen Wu temple|Wen Wu temple]] * [[National Revolutionary Martyrs' Shrine]] * [[Eternal Spring Shrine]] * [[:en:Chinese Cultural Renaissance|Chinese Cultural Renaissance]] * [[Ancestral shrine]] * [[:en:Gallant Garden|Gallant Garden]] * [[Gokoku Shrines]] * [[:en:Tomb of the Unknown Soldier|Tomb of the Unknown Soldier]] * [[:en:Arlington National Cemetery|Arlington National Cemetery]] * [[:en:Valhalla|Valhalla]] (home to the souls of fallen warriors in Scandinavian mythology) * [[:en:Walhalla (memorial)|Walhalla Shrine]] (a hall of fame in Germany honoring "commendable and honorable Germans") * [[Eternal Spring Shrine]] * [[The common end of myriad good deeds]] * [[:en:Greek hero cult|Greek hero cult]] == Notes == {{Notelist}} == References == {{Reflist}} == External Links == * [http://www.okinawa-gokoku.jp/ Official website of Okinawa Prefecture Gokoku Shrine] {{Shinto Horizontal}} {{Shinto-stub}} {{DEFAULTSORT:Okinawa Prefecture Gokoku Shrine}} [[Category:Shinto shrines in Okinawa Prefecture]] [[Category:Naha]] [[Category:Gokoku shrines]] [[Category:Independent Shinto shrines]] [[Category:Ken-sha]] [[Category:Buildings and structures in Naha]] [[Category:History of Okinawa Prefecture]] {{Draft categories| [[Category:Gokoku shrines]] [[Category:1875 establishments]] [[Category:Shinto shrines in Okinawa Prefecture]] [[Category:original pages]]{{Gokoku shrines}} [[Category:Gokoku shrines]] (Formerly ''[[Shokonsha]]'') | leadership = | website = | architecture_style = | founded_by = | established = | date_destroyed = | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | name = Okinawa Prefecture Gokoku Shrine | image = [[File:Okinawaken Gokoku Shrine haiden.jpg|250px|Okinawa Prefecture Gokoku Shrine Haiden]]
Haiden | coordinates = {{Coord|26|12|11.6|N|127|40|33.6|E|scale:25000_region:JP-47_type:landmark|display=inline,title}} | location = 奥武山町44, Naha City, Okinawa Prefecture, Japan | deity = * Local war dead enshrined at {{ill|Yasukuni Shrine|ja|靖国神社}} * War dead from mainland Japan who perished during the {{ill|Battle of Okinawa|ja|沖縄戦}} * Civilian victims and student casualties of the war | established = 1936 (Shōwa 11) | festivals = April 23, October 23 }} '''Okinawa Prefecture Gokoku Shrine''' ({{nihongo|沖縄県護国神社|Okinawaken Gokoku Jinja}}) is a [[Shinto shrine]] located within {{ill|Onoyama Park|ja|奥武山公園}}, {{ill|Naha|ja|那覇市}}, Okinawa Prefecture, Japan. The shrine is an independent religious institution not affiliated with [[Jinja Honcho]]. {{Interlanguage link|{{PAGENAME}}|ja|沖縄県護国神社}} is a [[Shinto shrine]] located in [[Japan]]. It is a [[Gokoku Shrines|Gokoku Shrine]], or a shrine dedicated to war dead.{{Efn|Is this [[WP:BLUE]] considering that the shrine is literally named as such?}} Such shrines were made to serve to enshrine the war dead, and they were all considered "branches" of [[Yasukuni Shrine]]. They were renamed from [[Shōkonsha|Shokonsha]] in 1939.{{Cite journal |last=TAKAYAMA |first=K. PETER |date=1990 |title=Enshrinement and Persistency of Japanese Religion |url=https://www.jstor.org/stable/23917081 |journal=Journal of Church and State |volume=32 |issue=3 |pages=527–547 |issn=0021-969X}} The Okinawa Gokoku-jinja Shrine also enshrines the dead of the [[Battle of Okinawa]], including ordinary residents, schoolchildren in distress, and civilian war dead.[[護国神社#山中|山中 2013]]、212頁{{Cite web |date=2022-03-08 |title=沖縄県護国神社について｜初詣、ご祈願、地鎮祭は、沖縄県護国神社 |url=https://www.okinawa-gokoku.jp/about.php |url-status=dead |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20220308190702/https://www.okinawa-gokoku.jp/about.php |archive-date=8 March 2022 |access-date=2022-03-08 |website=}} In addition, about 10,000 mobilized students and female volunteer corps members who were victims of the [[Atomic bomb]] are also enshrined as deities at [[Hiroshima Gokoku Shrine]].[[護国神社#山中|山中 2013]]、154頁{{Cite web |date=2021-01-20 |title=由緒 {{!}} 広島護國神社 |url=https://www.h-gokoku.or.jp/yuisho/ |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20210120233005/https://www.h-gokoku.or.jp/yuisho/ |archive-date=2021-01-20 |access-date=2022-03-08}} The main hall burned down during the [[Pacific War]] and was reconstructed in 1959.{{Cite book |last=Takenaka |first=Akiko |url=http://dx.doi.org/10.1515/9780824856939 |title=Yasukuni Shrine |date=2017-10-16 |publisher=University of Hawaii Press |isbn=978-0-8248-5693-9 |pages=158}} == Enshrined Deities == The shrine enshrines spirits of war dead from Okinawa Prefecture and mainland Japan, including civilians and student casualties who perished during the {{ill|Battle of Okinawa|ja|沖縄戦}}. As of October 2005, the shrine enshrines 178,689 spirits, the highest number among {{ill|Gokoku shrines|ja|護国神社}} nationwide. == History == [[File:Okinawaken_Gogoku_Shrine_torii.jpeg|thumb|right|170px|Torii gate]] The shrine traces its origins to a {{ill|Shōkonsha|ja|招魂社}} established in 1936 to honor those who died in service to Japan during conflicts such as the {{ill|First Sino-Japanese War|ja|日清戦争}} and the {{ill|Russo-Japanese War|ja|日露戦争}}.加治順人『沖縄の神社』p115　ひるぎ社、2000年10月30日 In 1940, it was designated as a Gokoku Shrine by the Ministry of Home Affairs and renamed "Okinawa Prefecture Gokoku Shrine."『全國護國神社會五十年史』（1997年3月1日、全國護國神社會発行）287頁。 The shrine was destroyed during the {{ill|Battle of Okinawa|ja|沖縄戦}} in April 1945. A temporary shrine was constructed on April 26, 1959. In the same year, relics of spirits from the {{ill|Yasukuni Shrine|ja|靖国神社}} were transferred to the shrine, honoring mainland Japanese who died in the Battle of Okinawa. The current main shrine building was completed in October 1965, with the relocation ceremony held on November 19 and the restoration commemorative festival on November 20.加治順人『沖縄の神社』p143　ひるぎ社、2000年10月30日 In 1980, a Kaguraden (dance hall) was added to the grounds.{{DEFAULTSORT:Okinawa Prefecture Gokoku Shrine}} [[Category:Shinto shrines in Okinawa Prefecture]] They were renamed from [[Shōkonsha|Shokonsha]] in 1939.{{Cite journal |last=TAKAYAMA |first=K. PETER |date=1990 |title=Enshrinement and Persistency of Japanese Religion |url=https://www.jstor.org/stable/23917081 |journal=Journal of Church and State |volume=32 |issue=3 |pages=527–547 |issn=0021-969X}} The Okinawa Gokoku-jinja Shrine also enshrines the dead of the [[Battle of Okinawa]], including ordinary residents, schoolchildren in distress, and civilian war dead.[[護国神社#山中|山中 2013]]、212頁{{Cite web |date=2022-03-08 |title=沖縄県護国神社について｜初詣、ご祈願、地鎮祭は、沖縄県護国神社 |url=https://www.okinawa-gokoku.jp/about.php |url-status=dead |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20220308190702/https://www.okinawa-gokoku.jp/about.php |archive-date=8 March 2022 |access-date=2022-03-08 |website=}} In addition, about 10,000 mobilized students and female volunteer corps members who were victims of the [[Atomic bomb]] are also enshrined as deities at [[Hiroshima Gokoku Shrine]].[[護国神社#山中|山中 2013]]、154頁{{Cite web |date=2021-01-20 |title=由緒 {{!}} 広島護國神社 |url=https://www.h-gokoku.or.jp/yuisho/ |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20210120233005/https://www.h-gokoku.or.jp/yuisho/ |archive-date=2021-01-20 |access-date=2022-03-08}} The main hall burned down during the [[Pacific War]] and was reconstructed in 1959.{{Cite book |last=Takenaka |first=Akiko |url=http://dx.doi.org/10.1515/9780824856939 |title=Yasukuni Shrine |date=2017-10-16 |publisher=University of Hawaii Press |isbn=978-0-8248-5693-9 |pages=158}} == Enshrined Deities == The shrine enshrines spirits of war dead from Okinawa Prefecture and mainland Japan, including civilians and student casualties who perished during the {{ill|Battle of Okinawa|ja|沖縄戦}}. As of October 2005, the shrine enshrines 178,689 spirits, the highest number among {{ill|Gokoku shrines|ja|護国神社}} nationwide. == History == [[File:Okinawaken_Gogoku_Shrine_torii.jpeg|thumb|right|170px|Torii gate]] The shrine traces its origins to a {{ill|Shōkonsha|ja|招魂社}} established in 1936 to honor those who died in service to Japan during conflicts such as the {{ill|First Sino-Japanese War|ja|日清戦争}} and the {{ill|Russo-Japanese War|ja|日露戦争}}.加治順人『沖縄の神社』p115　ひるぎ社、2000年10月30日 In 1940, it was designated as a Gokoku Shrine by the Ministry of Home Affairs and renamed "Okinawa Prefecture Gokoku Shrine."『全國護國神社會五十年史』（1997年3月1日、全國護國神社會発行）287頁。 The shrine was destroyed during the {{ill|Battle of Okinawa|ja|沖縄戦}} in April 1945. A temporary shrine was constructed on April 26, 1959. In the same year, relics of spirits from the {{ill|Yasukuni Shrine|ja|靖国神社}} were transferred to the shrine, honoring mainland Japanese who died in the Battle of Okinawa. The current main shrine building was completed in October 1965, with the relocation ceremony held on November 19 and the restoration commemorative festival on November 20.加治順人『沖縄の神社』p143　ひるぎ社、2000年10月30日 In 1980, a Kaguraden (dance hall) was added to the grounds.{{DEFAULTSORT:Okinawa Prefecture Gokoku Shrine}} [[Category:Shinto shrines in Okinawa Prefecture]] [[Category:Naha]] [[Category:Gokoku shrines]] [[Category:Independent Shinto shrines]] [[Category:Ken-sha]] [[Category:Buildings and structures in Naha]] [[Category:History of Okinawa Prefecture]] == See Also == * [[Controversies surrounding Yasukuni Shrine]] * [[Martyrs' shrines (China)|Martyr's Shrines]] * [[:en:Martial temple|Martial temple]] and [[:en:Wen Wu temple|Wen Wu temple]] * [[National Revolutionary Martyrs' Shrine]] * [[Eternal Spring Shrine]] * [[:en:Chinese Cultural Renaissance|Chinese Cultural Renaissance]] * [[Ancestral shrine]] * [[:en:Gallant Garden|Gallant Garden]] * [[Gokoku Shrines]] * [[:en:Tomb of the Unknown Soldier|Tomb of the Unknown Soldier]] * [[:en:Arlington National Cemetery|Arlington National Cemetery]] * [[:en:Valhalla|Valhalla]] (home to the souls of fallen warriors in Scandinavian mythology) * [[:en:Walhalla (memorial)|Walhalla Shrine]] (a hall of fame in Germany honoring "commendable and honorable Germans") * [[Eternal Spring Shrine]] * [[The common end of myriad good deeds]] * [[:en:Greek hero cult|Greek hero cult]] == Notes == {{Notelist}} == References == {{Reflist}} == External Links == * [http://www.okinawa-gokoku.jp/ Official website of Okinawa Prefecture Gokoku Shrine] {{Shinto Horizontal}} {{Shinto-stub}} {{DEFAULTSORT:Okinawa Prefecture Gokoku Shrine}} [[Category:Shinto shrines in Okinawa Prefecture]] [[Category:Naha]] (Formerly ''[[Shokonsha]]'') | leadership = | website = | architecture_style = | founded_by = | established = | date_destroyed = | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | name = Okinawa Prefecture Gokoku Shrine | image = [[File:Okinawaken Gokoku Shrine haiden.jpg|250px|Okinawa Prefecture Gokoku Shrine Haiden]]
Haiden | coordinates = {{Coord|26|12|11.6|N|127|40|33.6|E|scale:25000_region:JP-47_type:landmark|display=inline,title}} | location = 奥武山町44, Naha City, Okinawa Prefecture, Japan | deity = * Local war dead enshrined at {{ill|Yasukuni Shrine|ja|靖国神社}} * War dead from mainland Japan who perished during the {{ill|Battle of Okinawa|ja|沖縄戦}} * Civilian victims and student casualties of the war | established = 1936 (Shōwa 11) | festivals = April 23, October 23 }} '''Okinawa Prefecture Gokoku Shrine''' ({{nihongo|沖縄県護国神社|Okinawaken Gokoku Jinja}}) is a [[Shinto shrine]] located within {{ill|Onoyama Park|ja|奥武山公園}}, {{ill|Naha|ja|那覇市}}, Okinawa Prefecture, Japan. The shrine is an independent religious institution not affiliated with [[Jinja Honcho]]. {{Interlanguage link|{{PAGENAME}}|ja|沖縄県護国神社}} is a [[Shinto shrine]] located in [[Japan]]. It is a [[Gokoku Shrines|Gokoku Shrine]], or a shrine dedicated to war dead.{{Efn|Is this [[WP:BLUE]] considering that the shrine is literally named as such?}} Such shrines were made to serve to enshrine the war dead, and they were all considered "branches" of [[Yasukuni Shrine]]. They were renamed from [[Shōkonsha|Shokonsha]] in 1939.{{Cite journal |last=TAKAYAMA |first=K. PETER |date=1990 |title=Enshrinement and Persistency of Japanese Religion |url=https://www.jstor.org/stable/23917081 |journal=Journal of Church and State |volume=32 |issue=3 |pages=527–547 |issn=0021-969X}} The Okinawa Gokoku-jinja Shrine also enshrines the dead of the [[Battle of Okinawa]], including ordinary residents, schoolchildren in distress, and civilian war dead.[[護国神社#山中|山中 2013]]、212頁{{Cite web |date=2022-03-08 |title=沖縄県護国神社について｜初詣、ご祈願、地鎮祭は、沖縄県護国神社 |url=https://www.okinawa-gokoku.jp/about.php |url-status=dead |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20220308190702/https://www.okinawa-gokoku.jp/about.php |archive-date=8 March 2022 |access-date=2022-03-08 |website=}} In addition, about 10,000 mobilized students and female volunteer corps members who were victims of the [[Atomic bomb]] are also enshrined as deities at [[Hiroshima Gokoku Shrine]].[[護国神社#山中|山中 2013]]、154頁{{Cite web |date=2021-01-20 |title=由緒 {{!}} 広島護國神社 |url=https://www.h-gokoku.or.jp/yuisho/ |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20210120233005/https://www.h-gokoku.or.jp/yuisho/ |archive-date=2021-01-20 |access-date=2022-03-08}} The main hall burned down during the [[Pacific War]] and was reconstructed in 1959.{{Cite book |last=Takenaka |first=Akiko |url=http://dx.doi.org/10.1515/9780824856939 |title=Yasukuni Shrine |date=2017-10-16 |publisher=University of Hawaii Press |isbn=978-0-8248-5693-9 |pages=158}} == Enshrined Deities == The shrine enshrines spirits of war dead from Okinawa Prefecture and mainland Japan, including civilians and student casualties who perished during the {{ill|Battle of Okinawa|ja|沖縄戦}}. A temporary shrine was constructed on April 26, 1959. In the same year, relics of spirits from the {{ill|Yasukuni Shrine|ja|靖国神社}} were transferred to the shrine, honoring mainland Japanese who died in the Battle of Okinawa. The current main shrine building was completed in October 1965, with the relocation ceremony held on November 19 and the restoration commemorative festival on November 20.加治順人『沖縄の神社』p143　ひるぎ社、2000年10月30日 In 1980, a Kaguraden (dance hall) was added to the grounds.{{DEFAULTSORT:Okinawa Prefecture Gokoku Shrine}} [[Category:Shinto shrines in Okinawa Prefecture]] They were renamed from [[Shōkonsha|Shokonsha]] in 1939.{{Cite journal |last=TAKAYAMA |first=K. PETER |date=1990 |title=Enshrinement and Persistency of Japanese Religion |url=https://www.jstor.org/stable/23917081 |journal=Journal of Church and State |volume=32 |issue=3 |pages=527–547 |issn=0021-969X}} The Okinawa Gokoku-jinja Shrine also enshrines the dead of the [[Battle of Okinawa]], including ordinary residents, schoolchildren in distress, and civilian war dead.[[護国神社#山中|山中 2013]]、212頁{{Cite web |date=2022-03-08 |title=沖縄県護国神社について｜初詣、ご祈願、地鎮祭は、沖縄県護国神社 |url=https://www.okinawa-gokoku.jp/about.php |url-status=dead |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20220308190702/https://www.okinawa-gokoku.jp/about.php |archive-date=8 March 2022 |access-date=2022-03-08 |website=}} In addition, about 10,000 mobilized students and female volunteer corps members who were victims of the [[Atomic bomb]] are also enshrined as deities at [[Hiroshima Gokoku Shrine]].[[護国神社#山中|山中 2013]]、154頁{{Cite web |date=2021-01-20 |title=由緒 {{!}} 広島護國神社 |url=https://www.h-gokoku.or.jp/yuisho/ |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20210120233005/https://www.h-gokoku.or.jp/yuisho/ |archive-date=2021-01-20 |access-date=2022-03-08}} The main hall burned down during the [[Pacific War]] and was reconstructed in 1959.{{Cite book |last=Takenaka |first=Akiko |url=http://dx.doi.org/10.1515/9780824856939 |title=Yasukuni Shrine |date=2017-10-16 |publisher=University of Hawaii Press |isbn=978-0-8248-5693-9 |pages=158}} == Enshrined Deities == The shrine enshrines spirits of war dead from Okinawa Prefecture and mainland Japan, including civilians and student casualties who perished during the {{ill|Battle of Okinawa|ja|沖縄戦}}. As of October 2005, the shrine enshrines 178,689 spirits, the highest number among {{ill|Gokoku shrines|ja|護国神社}} nationwide. == History == [[File:Okinawaken_Gogoku_Shrine_torii.jpeg|thumb|right|170px|Torii gate]] The shrine traces its origins to a {{ill|Shōkonsha|ja|招魂社}} established in 1936 to honor those who died in service to Japan during conflicts such as the {{ill|First Sino-Japanese War|ja|日清戦争}} and the {{ill|Russo-Japanese War|ja|日露戦争}}.加治順人『沖縄の神社』p115　ひるぎ社、2000年10月30日 In 1940, it was designated as a Gokoku Shrine by the Ministry of Home Affairs and renamed "Okinawa Prefecture Gokoku Shrine."『全國護國神社會五十年史』（1997年3月1日、全國護國神社會発行）287頁。 The shrine was destroyed during the {{ill|Battle of Okinawa|ja|沖縄戦}} in April 1945. A temporary shrine was constructed on April 26, 1959. In the same year, relics of spirits from the {{ill|Yasukuni Shrine|ja|靖国神社}} were transferred to the shrine, honoring mainland Japanese who died in the Battle of Okinawa. The current main shrine building was completed in October 1965, with the relocation ceremony held on November 19 and the restoration commemorative festival on November 20.加治順人『沖縄の神社』p143　ひるぎ社、2000年10月30日 In 1980, a Kaguraden (dance hall) was added to the grounds.{{DEFAULTSORT:Okinawa Prefecture Gokoku Shrine}} [[Category:Shinto shrines in Okinawa Prefecture]] [[Category:Naha]] [[Category:Gokoku shrines]] [[Category:Independent Shinto shrines]] [[Category:Ken-sha]] [[Category:Buildings and structures in Naha]] [[Category:History of Okinawa Prefecture]] == See Also == * [[Controversies surrounding Yasukuni Shrine]] * [[Martyrs' shrines (China)|Martyr's Shrines]] * [[:en:Martial temple|Martial temple]] and [[:en:Wen Wu temple|Wen Wu temple]] * [[National Revolutionary Martyrs' Shrine]] * [[Eternal Spring Shrine]] * [[:en:Chinese Cultural Renaissance|Chinese Cultural Renaissance]] * [[Ancestral shrine]] * [[:en:Gallant Garden|Gallant Garden]] * [[Gokoku Shrines]] * [[:en:Tomb of the Unknown Soldier|Tomb of the Unknown Soldier]] * [[:en:Arlington National Cemetery|Arlington National Cemetery]] * [[:en:Valhalla|Valhalla]] (home to the souls of fallen warriors in Scandinavian mythology) * [[:en:Walhalla (memorial)|Walhalla Shrine]] (a hall of fame in Germany honoring "commendable and honorable Germans") * [[Eternal Spring Shrine]] * [[The common end of myriad good deeds]] * [[:en:Greek hero cult|Greek hero cult]] == Notes == {{Notelist}} == References == {{Reflist}} == External Links == * [http://www.okinawa-gokoku.jp/ Official website of Okinawa Prefecture Gokoku Shrine] {{Shinto Horizontal}} {{Shinto-stub}} {{DEFAULTSORT:Okinawa Prefecture Gokoku Shrine}} [[Category:Shinto shrines in Okinawa Prefecture]] [[Category:Naha]] [[Category:Gokoku shrines]] [[Category:Independent Shinto shrines]] [[Category:Ken-sha]] [[Category:Buildings and structures in Naha]] [[Category:History of Okinawa Prefecture]] {{Draft categories| [[Category:Gokoku shrines]] [[Category:1875 establishments]] [[Category:Shinto shrines in Okinawa Prefecture]] [[Category:ZZZZZZZZZ Unlinked]][[category:unsorted completion categories]] {{Gokoku shrines}} [[Category:Gokoku shrines]] [[Category:bump]] [[Category:attempting to force efresh]] [[Category:refreshing the redirects]] [[Category:bump-01-18]] [[Category:Unsorted completion categories]][[Category:ZZZZZZZZZ Unlinked]][[Category:Unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:bump1-19]] [[Category:refreshing categories imported on 2024-01-19]] [[ja:沖縄県護国神社]] }} {{⚠️🚨COPIED (Formerly ''[[Shokonsha]]'') | leadership = | website = | architecture_style = | founded_by = | established = | date_destroyed = | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | name = Okinawa Prefecture Gokoku Shrine | image = [[File:Okinawaken Gokoku Shrine haiden.jpg|250px|Okinawa Prefecture Gokoku Shrine Haiden]]
Haiden | coordinates = {{Coord|26|12|11.6|N|127|40|33.6|E|scale:25000_region:JP-47_type:landmark|display=inline,title}} | location = 奥武山町44, Naha City, Okinawa Prefecture, Japan | deity = * Local war dead enshrined at {{ill|Yasukuni Shrine|ja|靖国神社}} * War dead from mainland Japan who perished during the {{ill|Battle of Okinawa|ja|沖縄戦}} * Civilian victims and student casualties of the war | established = 1936 (Shōwa 11) | festivals = April 23, October 23 }} '''Okinawa Prefecture Gokoku Shrine''' ({{nihongo|沖縄県護国神社|Okinawaken Gokoku Jinja}}) is a [[Shinto shrine]] located within {{ill|Onoyama Park|ja|奥武山公園}}, {{ill|Naha|ja|那覇市}}, Okinawa Prefecture, Japan. The shrine is an independent religious institution not affiliated with [[Jinja Honcho]]. {{Interlanguage link|{{PAGENAME}}|ja|沖縄県護国神社}} is a [[Shinto shrine]] located in [[Japan]]. It is a [[Gokoku Shrines|Gokoku Shrine]], or a shrine dedicated to war dead.{{Efn|Is this [[WP:BLUE]] considering that the shrine is literally named as such?}} Such shrines were made to serve to enshrine the war dead, and they were all considered "branches" of [[Yasukuni Shrine]]. They were renamed from [[Shōkonsha|Shokonsha]] in 1939.{{Cite journal |last=TAKAYAMA |first=K. PETER |date=1990 |title=Enshrinement and Persistency of Japanese Religion |url=https://www.jstor.org/stable/23917081 |journal=Journal of Church and State |volume=32 |issue=3 |pages=527–547 |issn=0021-969X}} The Okinawa Gokoku-jinja Shrine also enshrines the dead of the [[Battle of Okinawa]], including ordinary residents, schoolchildren in distress, and civilian war dead.[[護国神社#山中|山中 2013]]、212頁{{Cite web |date=2022-03-08 |title=沖縄県護国神社について｜初詣、ご祈願、地鎮祭は、沖縄県護国神社 |url=https://www.okinawa-gokoku.jp/about.php |url-status=dead |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20220308190702/https://www.okinawa-gokoku.jp/about.php |archive-date=8 March 2022 |access-date=2022-03-08 |website=}} In addition, about 10,000 mobilized students and female volunteer corps members who were victims of the [[Atomic bomb]] are also enshrined as deities at [[Hiroshima Gokoku Shrine]].[[護国神社#山中|山中 2013]]、154頁{{Cite web |date=2021-01-20 |title=由緒 {{!}} 広島護國神社 |url=https://www.h-gokoku.or.jp/yuisho/ |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20210120233005/https://www.h-gokoku.or.jp/yuisho/ |archive-date=2021-01-20 |access-date=2022-03-08}} The main hall burned down during the [[Pacific War]] and was reconstructed in 1959.{{Cite book |last=Takenaka |first=Akiko |url=http://dx.doi.org/10.1515/9780824856939 |title=Yasukuni Shrine |date=2017-10-16 |publisher=University of Hawaii Press |isbn=978-0-8248-5693-9 |pages=158}} == Enshrined Deities == The shrine enshrines spirits of war dead from Okinawa Prefecture and mainland Japan, including civilians and student casualties who perished during the {{ill|Battle of Okinawa|ja|沖縄戦}}. As of October 2005, the shrine enshrines 178,689 spirits, the highest number among {{ill|Gokoku shrines|ja|護国神社}} nationwide. == History == [[File:Okinawaken_Gogoku_Shrine_torii.jpeg|thumb|right|170px|Torii gate]] The shrine traces its origins to a {{ill|Shōkonsha|ja|招魂社}} established in 1936 to honor those who died in service to Japan during conflicts such as the {{ill|First Sino-Japanese War|ja|日清戦争}} and the {{ill|Russo-Japanese War|ja|日露戦争}}.加治順人『沖縄の神社』p115　ひるぎ社、2000年10月30日 In 1940, it was designated as a Gokoku Shrine by the Ministry of Home Affairs and renamed "Okinawa Prefecture Gokoku Shrine."『全國護國神社會五十年史』（1997年3月1日、全國護國神社會発行）287頁。 The shrine was destroyed during the {{ill|Battle of Okinawa|ja|沖縄戦}} in April 1945. A temporary shrine was constructed on April 26, 1959. In the same year, relics of spirits from the {{ill|Yasukuni Shrine|ja|靖国神社}} were transferred to the shrine, honoring mainland Japanese who died in the Battle of Okinawa. The current main shrine building was completed in October 1965, with the relocation ceremony held on November 19 and the restoration commemorative festival on November 20.加治順人『沖縄の神社』p143　ひるぎ社、2000年10月30日 In 1980, a Kaguraden (dance hall) was added to the grounds.{{DEFAULTSORT:Okinawa Prefecture Gokoku Shrine}} [[Category:Shinto shrines in Okinawa Prefecture]] It is a [[Gokoku Shrines|Gokoku Shrine]], or a shrine dedicated to war dead.{{Efn|Is this [[WP:BLUE]] considering that the shrine is literally named as such?}} Such shrines were made to serve to enshrine the war dead, and they were all considered "branches" of [[Yasukuni Shrine]]. They were renamed from [[Shōkonsha|Shokonsha]] in 1939.{{Cite journal |last=TAKAYAMA |first=K. PETER |date=1990 |title=Enshrinement and Persistency of Japanese Religion |url=https://www.jstor.org/stable/23917081 |journal=Journal of Church and State |volume=32 |issue=3 |pages=527–547 |issn=0021-969X}} The Okinawa Gokoku-jinja Shrine also enshrines the dead of the [[Battle of Okinawa]], including ordinary residents, schoolchildren in distress, and civilian war dead.[[護国神社#山中|山中 2013]]、212頁{{Cite web |date=2022-03-08 |title=沖縄県護国神社について｜初詣、ご祈願、地鎮祭は、沖縄県護国神社 |url=https://www.okinawa-gokoku.jp/about.php |url-status=dead |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20220308190702/https://www.okinawa-gokoku.jp/about.php |archive-date=8 March 2022 |access-date=2022-03-08 |website=}} In addition, about 10,000 mobilized students and female volunteer corps members who were victims of the [[Atomic bomb]] are also enshrined as deities at [[Hiroshima Gokoku Shrine]].[[護国神社#山中|山中 2013]]、154頁{{Cite web |date=2021-01-20 |title=由緒 {{!}} 広島護國神社 |url=https://www.h-gokoku.or.jp/yuisho/ |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20210120233005/https://www.h-gokoku.or.jp/yuisho/ |archive-date=2021-01-20 |access-date=2022-03-08}} The main hall burned down during the [[Pacific War]] and was reconstructed in 1959.{{Cite book |last=Takenaka |first=Akiko |url=http://dx.doi.org/10.1515/9780824856939 |title=Yasukuni Shrine |date=2017-10-16 |publisher=University of Hawaii Press |isbn=978-0-8248-5693-9 |pages=158}} == Enshrined Deities == The shrine enshrines spirits of war dead from Okinawa Prefecture and mainland Japan, including civilians and student casualties who perished during the {{ill|Battle of Okinawa|ja|沖縄戦}}. As of October 2005, the shrine enshrines 178,689 spirits, the highest number among {{ill|Gokoku shrines|ja|護国神社}} nationwide. == History == [[File:Okinawaken_Gogoku_Shrine_torii.jpeg|thumb|right|170px|Torii gate]] The shrine traces its origins to a {{ill|Shōkonsha|ja|招魂社}} established in 1936 to honor those who died in service to Japan during conflicts such as the {{ill|First Sino-Japanese War|ja|日清戦争}} and the {{ill|Russo-Japanese War|ja|日露戦争}}.加治順人『沖縄の神社』p115　ひるぎ社、2000年10月30日 In 1940, it was designated as a Gokoku Shrine by the Ministry of Home Affairs and renamed "Okinawa Prefecture Gokoku Shrine."『全國護國神社會五十年史』（1997年3月1日、全國護國神社會発行）287頁。 The shrine was destroyed during the {{ill|Battle of Okinawa|ja|沖縄戦}} in April 1945. A temporary shrine was constructed on April 26, 1959. In the same year, relics of spirits from the {{ill|Yasukuni Shrine|ja|靖国神社}} were transferred to the shrine, honoring mainland Japanese who died in the Battle of Okinawa. The current main shrine building was completed in October 1965, with the relocation ceremony held on November 19 and the restoration commemorative festival on November 20.加治順人『沖縄の神社』p143　ひるぎ社、2000年10月30日 In 1980, a Kaguraden (dance hall) was added to the grounds.{{DEFAULTSORT:Okinawa Prefecture Gokoku Shrine}} [[Category:Shinto shrines in Okinawa Prefecture]] [[Category:Naha]] [[Category:Gokoku shrines]] [[Category:Independent Shinto shrines]] [[Category:Ken-sha]] [[Category:Buildings and structures in Naha]] [[Category:History of Okinawa Prefecture]] == See Also == * [[Controversies surrounding Yasukuni Shrine]] * [[Martyrs' shrines (China)|Martyr's Shrines]] * [[:en:Martial temple|Martial temple]] and [[:en:Wen Wu temple|Wen Wu temple]] * [[National Revolutionary Martyrs' Shrine]] * [[Eternal Spring Shrine]] * [[:en:Chinese Cultural Renaissance|Chinese Cultural Renaissance]] * [[Ancestral shrine]] * [[:en:Gallant Garden|Gallant Garden]] * [[Gokoku Shrines]] * [[:en:Tomb of the Unknown Soldier|Tomb of the Unknown Soldier]] * [[:en:Arlington National Cemetery|Arlington National Cemetery]] * [[:en:Valhalla|Valhalla]] (home to the souls of fallen warriors in Scandinavian mythology) * [[:en:Walhalla (memorial)|Walhalla Shrine]] (a hall of fame in Germany honoring "commendable and honorable Germans") * [[Eternal Spring Shrine]] * [[The common end of myriad good deeds]] * [[:en:Greek hero cult|Greek hero cult]] == Notes == {{Notelist}} == References == {{Reflist}} == External Links == * [http://www.okinawa-gokoku.jp/ Official website of Okinawa Prefecture Gokoku Shrine] {{Shinto Horizontal}} {{Shinto-stub}} {{DEFAULTSORT:Okinawa Prefecture Gokoku Shrine}} [[Category:Shinto shrines in Okinawa Prefecture]] [[Category:Naha]] [[Category:Gokoku shrines]] [[Category:Independent Shinto shrines]] [[Category:Ken-sha]] [[Category:Buildings and structures in Naha]] [[Category:History of Okinawa Prefecture]] {{Draft categories| shrines}} [[Category:Gokoku shrines]] [[Category:bump]] [[Category:attempting to force efresh]] [[Category:refreshing the redirects]] [[Category:bump-01-18]] [[Category:Unsorted completion categories]][[Category:ZZZZZZZZZ Unlinked]][[Category:Unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:bump1-19]] [[Category:refreshing categories imported on 2024-01-19]] [[ja:沖縄県護国神社]] }} {{⚠️🚨COPIED from EverybodyWiki ❗❕⚠️😡😤Please respect Licence CC-BY-SA ❗}} {{Source Wikipedia}}

It is a [[Gokoku Shrines|Gokoku Shrine]], or a shrine dedicated to war dead.{{Efn|Is this [[WP:BLUE]] considering that the shrine is literally named as such?}} Such shrines were made to serve to enshrine the war dead, and they were all considered "branches" of [[Yasukuni Shrine]]. They were renamed from [[Shōkonsha|Shokonsha]] in 1939.{{Cite journal |last=TAKAYAMA |first=K. PETER |date=1990 |title=Enshrinement and Persistency of Japanese Religion |url=https://www.jstor.org/stable/23917081 |journal=Journal of Church and State |volume=32 |issue=3 |pages=527–547 |issn=0021-969X}} The Okinawa Gokoku-jinja Shrine also enshrines the dead of the [[Battle of Okinawa]], including ordinary residents, schoolchildren in distress, and civilian war dead.[[護国神社#山中|山中 2013]]、212頁{{Cite web |date=2022-03-08 |title=沖縄県護国神社について｜初詣、ご祈願、地鎮祭は、沖縄県護国神社 |url=https://www.okinawa-gokoku.jp/about.php |url-status=dead |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20220308190702/https://www.okinawa-gokoku.jp/about.php |archive-date=8 March 2022 |access-date=2022-03-08 |website=}} In addition, about 10,000 mobilized students and female volunteer corps members who were victims of the [[Atomic bomb]] are also enshrined as deities at [[Hiroshima Gokoku Shrine]].[[護国神社#山中|山中 2013]]、154頁{{Cite web |date=2021-01-20 |title=由緒 {{!}} 広島護國神社 |url=https://www.h-gokoku.or.jp/yuisho/ |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20210120233005/https://www.h-gokoku.or.jp/yuisho/ |archive-date=2021-01-20 |access-date=2022-03-08}} The main hall burned down during the [[Pacific War]] and was reconstructed in 1959.{{Cite book |last=Takenaka |first=Akiko |url=http://dx.doi.org/10.1515/9780824856939 |title=Yasukuni Shrine |date=2017-10-16 |publisher=University of Hawaii Press |isbn=978-0-8248-5693-9 |pages=158}} == Enshrined Deities == The shrine enshrines spirits of war dead from Okinawa Prefecture and mainland Japan, including civilians and student casualties who perished during the {{ill|Battle of Okinawa|ja|沖縄戦}}. As of October 2005, the shrine enshrines 178,689 spirits, the highest number among {{ill|Gokoku shrines|ja|護国神社}} nationwide. == History == [[File:Okinawaken_Gogoku_Shrine_torii.jpeg|thumb|right|170px|Torii gate]] The shrine traces its origins to a {{ill|Shōkonsha|ja|招魂社}} established in 1936 to honor those who died in service to Japan during conflicts such as the {{ill|First Sino-Japanese War|ja|日清戦争}} and the {{ill|Russo-Japanese War|ja|日露戦争}}.加治順人『沖縄の神社』p115　ひるぎ社、2000年10月30日 In 1940, it was designated as a Gokoku Shrine by the Ministry of Home Affairs and renamed "Okinawa Prefecture Gokoku Shrine."『全國護國神社會五十年史』（1997年3月1日、全國護國神社會発行）287頁。 The shrine was destroyed during the {{ill|Battle of Okinawa|ja|沖縄戦}} in April 1945. A temporary shrine was constructed on April 26, 1959. In the same year, relics of spirits from the {{ill|Yasukuni Shrine|ja|靖国神社}} were transferred to the shrine, honoring mainland Japanese who died in the Battle of Okinawa. The current main shrine building was completed in October 1965, with the relocation ceremony held on November 19 and the restoration commemorative festival on November 20.加治順人『沖縄の神社』p143　ひるぎ社、2000年10月30日 In 1980, a Kaguraden (dance hall) was added to the grounds.{{DEFAULTSORT:Okinawa Prefecture Gokoku Shrine}} [[Category:Shinto shrines in Okinawa Prefecture]] [[Category:Naha]] [[Category:Gokoku shrines]] [[Category:Independent Shinto shrines]] [[Category:Ken-sha]] [[Category:Buildings and structures in Naha]] [[Category:History of Okinawa Prefecture]] == See Also == * [[Controversies surrounding Yasukuni Shrine]] * [[Martyrs' shrines (China)|Martyr's Shrines]] * [[:en:Martial temple|Martial temple]] and [[:en:Wen Wu temple|Wen Wu temple]] * [[National Revolutionary Martyrs' Shrine]] * [[Eternal Spring Shrine]] * [[:en:Chinese Cultural Renaissance|Chinese Cultural Renaissance]] * [[Ancestral shrine]] * [[:en:Gallant Garden|Gallant Garden]] * [[Gokoku Shrines]] * [[:en:Tomb of the Unknown Soldier|Tomb of the Unknown Soldier]] * [[:en:Arlington National Cemetery|Arlington National Cemetery]] * [[:en:Valhalla|Valhalla]] (home to the souls of fallen warriors in Scandinavian mythology) * [[:en:Walhalla (memorial)|Walhalla Shrine]] (a hall of fame in Germany honoring "commendable and honorable Germans") * [[Eternal Spring Shrine]] * [[The common end of myriad good deeds]] * [[:en:Greek hero cult|Greek hero cult]] == Notes == {{Notelist}} == References == {{Reflist}} == External Links == * [http://www.okinawa-gokoku.jp/ Official website of Okinawa Prefecture Gokoku Shrine] {{Shinto Horizontal}} {{Shinto-stub}} {{DEFAULTSORT:Okinawa Prefecture Gokoku Shrine}} [[Category:Shinto shrines in Okinawa Prefecture]] [[Category:Naha]] [[Category:Gokoku shrines]] [[Category:Independent Shinto shrines]] [[Category:Ken-sha]] [[Category:Buildings and structures in Naha]] [[Category:History of Okinawa Prefecture]] {{Draft categories| [[Category:Gokoku shrines]] [[Category:1875 establishments]] [[Category:Shinto shrines in Okinawa Prefecture]] [[Category:ZZZZZZZZZ Unlinked]][[category:unsorted completion categories]] {{Gokoku shrines}} [[Category:Gokoku shrines]] [[Category:bump]] [[Category:attempting to force efresh]] [[Category:refreshing the redirects]] [[Category:bump-01-18]] [[Category:Unsorted completion categories]][[Category:ZZZZZZZZZ Unlinked]][[Category:Unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:bump1-19]] [[Category:refreshing categories imported on 2024-01-19]] [[Category:refreshing imported pages]] [[Category:refreshing redirects 1-26]] [[Category:second attempt at refreshing on January 27 2024]][[Category:Okinawa Gokoku Shrine]][[Category:second attempt at refreshing on January 27 2024]] [[ja:沖縄県護国神社]] }} {{⚠️🚨COPIED from EverybodyWiki ❗❕⚠️😡😤Please respect Licence CC-BY-SA ❗}} {{Source Wikipedia}}

It is a [[Gokoku Shrines|Gokoku Shrine]], or a shrine dedicated to war dead.{{Efn|Is this [[WP:BLUE]] considering that the shrine is literally named as such?}} Such shrines were made to serve to enshrine the war dead, and they were all considered "branches" of [[Yasukuni Shrine]]. They were renamed from [[Shōkonsha|Shokonsha]] in 1939.{{Cite journal |last=TAKAYAMA |first=K. PETER |date=1990 |title=Enshrinement and Persistency of Japanese Religion |url=https://www.jstor.org/stable/23917081 |journal=Journal of Church and State |volume=32 |issue=3 |pages=527–547 |issn=0021-969X}} The Okinawa Gokoku-jinja Shrine also enshrines the dead of the [[Battle of Okinawa]], including ordinary residents, schoolchildren in distress, and civilian war dead.[[護国神社#山中|山中 2013]]、212頁{{Cite web |date=2022-03-08 |title=沖縄県護国神社について｜初詣、ご祈願、地鎮祭は、沖縄県護国神社 |url=https://www.okinawa-gokoku.jp/about.php |url-status=dead |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20220308190702/https://www.okinawa-gokoku.jp/about.php |archive-date=8 March 2022 |access-date=2022-03-08 |website=}} In addition, about 10,000 mobilized students and female volunteer corps members who were victims of the [[Atomic bomb]] are also enshrined as deities at [[Hiroshima Gokoku Shrine]].[[護国神社#山中|山中 2013]]、154頁{{Cite web |date=2021-01-20 |title=由緒 {{!}} 広島護國神社 |url=https://www.h-gokoku.or.jp/yuisho/ |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20210120233005/https://www.h-gokoku.or.jp/yuisho/ |archive-date=2021-01-20 |access-date=2022-03-08}} The main hall burned down during the [[Pacific War]] and was reconstructed in 1959.{{Cite book |last=Takenaka |first=Akiko |url=http://dx.doi.org/10.1515/9780824856939 |title=Yasukuni Shrine |date=2017-10-16 |publisher=University of Hawaii Press |isbn=978-0-8248-5693-9 |pages=158}} == Enshrined Deities == The shrine enshrines spirits of war dead from Okinawa Prefecture and mainland Japan, including civilians and student casualties who perished during the {{ill|Battle of Okinawa|ja|沖縄戦}}. As of October 2005, the shrine enshrines 178,689 spirits, the highest number among {{ill|Gokoku shrines|ja|護国神社}} nationwide. == History == [[File:Okinawaken_Gogoku_Shrine_torii.jpeg|thumb|right|170px|Torii gate]] The shrine traces its origins to a {{ill|Shōkonsha|ja|招魂社}} established in 1936 to honor those who died in service to Japan during conflicts such as the {{ill|First Sino-Japanese War|ja|日清戦争}} and the {{ill|Russo-Japanese War|ja|日露戦争}}.加治順人『沖縄の神社』p115　ひるぎ社、2000年10月30日 In 1940, it was designated as a Gokoku Shrine by the Ministry of Home Affairs and renamed "Okinawa Prefecture Gokoku Shrine."『全國護國神社會五十年史』（1997年3月1日、全國護國神社會発行）287頁。 The shrine was destroyed during the {{ill|Battle of Okinawa|ja|沖縄戦}} in April 1945. A temporary shrine was constructed on April 26, 1959. In the same year, relics of spirits from the {{ill|Yasukuni Shrine|ja|靖国神社}} were transferred to the shrine, honoring mainland Japanese who died in the Battle of Okinawa. The current main shrine building was completed in October 1965, with the relocation ceremony held on November 19 and the restoration commemorative festival on November 20.加治順人『沖縄の神社』p143　ひるぎ社、2000年10月30日 In 1980, a Kaguraden (dance hall) was added to the grounds.{{DEFAULTSORT:Okinawa Prefecture Gokoku Shrine}} [[Category:Shinto shrines in Okinawa Prefecture]] [[Category:Naha]] [[Category:Gokoku shrines]] [[Category:Independent Shinto shrines]] [[Category:Ken-sha]] [[Category:Buildings and structures in Naha]] [[Category:History of Okinawa Prefecture]] == See Also == * [[Controversies surrounding Yasukuni Shrine]] * [[Martyrs' shrines (China)|Martyr's Shrines]] * [[:en:Martial temple|Martial temple]] and [[:en:Wen Wu temple|Wen Wu temple]] * [[National Revolutionary Martyrs' Shrine]] * [[Eternal Spring Shrine]] * [[:en:Chinese Cultural Renaissance|Chinese Cultural Renaissance]] * [[Ancestral shrine]] * [[:en:Gallant Garden|Gallant Garden]] * [[Gokoku Shrines]] * [[:en:Tomb of the Unknown Soldier|Tomb of the Unknown Soldier]] * [[:en:Arlington National Cemetery|Arlington National Cemetery]] * [[:en:Valhalla|Valhalla]] (home to the souls of fallen warriors in Scandinavian mythology) * [[:en:Walhalla (memorial)|Walhalla Shrine]] (a hall of fame in Germany honoring "commendable and honorable Germans") * [[Eternal Spring Shrine]] * [[The common end of myriad good deeds]] * [[:en:Greek hero cult|Greek hero cult]] == Notes == {{Notelist}} == References == {{Reflist}} == External Links == * [http://www.okinawa-gokoku.jp/ Official website of Okinawa Prefecture Gokoku Shrine] {{Shinto Horizontal}} {{Shinto-stub}} {{DEFAULTSORT:Okinawa Prefecture Gokoku Shrine}} [[Category:Shinto shrines in Okinawa Prefecture]] [[Category:Naha]] [[Category:Gokoku shrines]] [[Category:Independent Shinto shrines]] [[Category:Ken-sha]] [[Category:Buildings and structures in Naha]] [[Category:History of Okinawa Prefecture]] {{Draft categories| refreshing on January 27 2024]] [[ja:沖縄県護国神社]] }} {{⚠️🚨COPIED from EverybodyWiki ❗❕⚠️😡😤Please respect Licence CC-BY-SA ❗}} {{Source Wikipedia}}

(Formerly ''[[Shokonsha]]'') | leadership = | website = | architecture_style = | founded_by = | established = | date_destroyed = | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | name = Okinawa Prefecture Gokoku Shrine | image = [[File:Okinawaken Gokoku Shrine haiden.jpg|250px|Okinawa Prefecture Gokoku Shrine Haiden]]
Haiden | coordinates = {{Coord|26|12|11.6|N|127|40|33.6|E|scale:25000_region:JP-47_type:landmark|display=inline,title}} | location = 奥武山町44, Naha City, Okinawa Prefecture, Japan | deity = * Local war dead enshrined at {{ill|Yasukuni Shrine|ja|靖国神社}} * War dead from mainland Japan who perished during the {{ill|Battle of Okinawa|ja|沖縄戦}} * Civilian victims and student casualties of the war | established = 1936 (Shōwa 11) | festivals = April 23, October 23 }} '''Okinawa Prefecture Gokoku Shrine''' ({{nihongo|沖縄県護国神社|Okinawaken Gokoku Jinja}}) is a [[Shinto shrine]] located within {{ill|Onoyama Park|ja|奥武山公園}}, {{ill|Naha|ja|那覇市}}, Okinawa Prefecture, Japan. The shrine is an independent religious institution not affiliated with [[Jinja Honcho]]. {{Interlanguage link|{{PAGENAME}}|ja|沖縄県護国神社}} is a [[Shinto shrine]] located in [[Japan]]. It is a [[Gokoku Shrines|Gokoku Shrine]], or a shrine dedicated to war dead.{{Efn|Is this [[WP:BLUE]] considering that the shrine is literally named as such?}} Such shrines were made to serve to enshrine the war dead, and they were all considered "branches" of [[Yasukuni Shrine]]. They were renamed from [[Shōkonsha|Shokonsha]] in 1939.{{Cite journal |last=TAKAYAMA |first=K. PETER |date=1990 |title=Enshrinement and Persistency of Japanese Religion |url=https://www.jstor.org/stable/23917081 |journal=Journal of Church and State |volume=32 |issue=3 |pages=527–547 |issn=0021-969X}} The Okinawa Gokoku-jinja Shrine also enshrines the dead of the [[Battle of Okinawa]], including ordinary residents, schoolchildren in distress, and civilian war dead.[[護国神社#山中|山中 2013]]、212頁{{Cite web |date=2022-03-08 |title=沖縄県護国神社について｜初詣、ご祈願、地鎮祭は、沖縄県護国神社 |url=https://www.okinawa-gokoku.jp/about.php |url-status=dead |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20220308190702/https://www.okinawa-gokoku.jp/about.php |archive-date=8 March 2022 |access-date=2022-03-08 |website=}} In addition, about 10,000 mobilized students and female volunteer corps members who were victims of the [[Atomic bomb]] are also enshrined as deities at [[Hiroshima Gokoku Shrine]].[[護国神社#山中|山中 2013]]、154頁{{Cite web |date=2021-01-20 |title=由緒 {{!}} 広島護國神社 |url=https://www.h-gokoku.or.jp/yuisho/ |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20210120233005/https://www.h-gokoku.or.jp/yuisho/ |archive-date=2021-01-20 |access-date=2022-03-08}} The main hall burned down during the [[Pacific War]] and was reconstructed in 1959.{{Cite book |last=Takenaka |first=Akiko |url=http://dx.doi.org/10.1515/9780824856939 |title=Yasukuni Shrine |date=2017-10-16 |publisher=University of Hawaii Press |isbn=978-0-8248-5693-9 |pages=158}} == Enshrined Deities == The shrine enshrines spirits of war dead from Okinawa Prefecture and mainland Japan, including civilians and student casualties who perished during the {{ill|Battle of Okinawa|ja|沖縄戦}}. As of October 2005, the shrine enshrines 178,689 spirits, the highest number among {{ill|Gokoku shrines|ja|護国神社}} nationwide. == History == [[File:Okinawaken_Gogoku_Shrine_torii.jpeg|thumb|right|170px|Torii gate]] The shrine traces its origins to a {{ill|Shōkonsha|ja|招魂社}} established in 1936 to honor those who died in service to Japan during conflicts such as the {{ill|First Sino-Japanese War|ja|日清戦争}} and the {{ill|Russo-Japanese War|ja|日露戦争}}.加治順人『沖縄の神社』p115　ひるぎ社、2000年10月30日 In 1940, it was designated as a Gokoku Shrine by the Ministry of Home Affairs and renamed "Okinawa Prefecture Gokoku Shrine."『全國護國神社會五十年史』（1997年3月1日、全國護國神社會発行）287頁。 The shrine was destroyed during the {{ill|Battle of Okinawa|ja|沖縄戦}} in April 1945. A temporary shrine was constructed on April 26, 1959. In the same year, relics of spirits from the {{ill|Yasukuni Shrine|ja|靖国神社}} were transferred to the shrine, honoring mainland Japanese who died in the Battle of Okinawa. The current main shrine building was completed in October 1965, with the relocation ceremony held on November 19 and the restoration commemorative festival on November 20.加治順人『沖縄の神社』p143　ひるぎ社、2000年10月30日 In 1980, a Kaguraden (dance hall) was added to the grounds.{{DEFAULTSORT:Okinawa Prefecture Gokoku Shrine}} [[Category:Shinto shrines in Okinawa Prefecture]] [[Category:Naha]] [[Category:Gokoku shrines]] [[Category:Independent Shinto shrines]] [[Category:Ken-sha]] [[Category:Buildings and structures in Naha]] [[Category:History of Okinawa Prefecture]] == Revolutionary Martyrs' Shrine]] * [[Eternal Spring Shrine]] * [[:en:Chinese Cultural Renaissance|Chinese Cultural Renaissance]] * [[Ancestral shrine]] * [[:en:Gallant Garden|Gallant Garden]] * [[Gokoku Shrines]] * [[:en:Tomb of the Unknown Soldier|Tomb of the Unknown Soldier]] * [[:en:Arlington National Cemetery|Arlington National Cemetery]] * [[:en:Valhalla|Valhalla]] (home to the souls of fallen warriors in Scandinavian mythology) * [[:en:Walhalla (memorial)|Walhalla Shrine]] (a hall of fame in Germany honoring "commendable and honorable Germans") * [[Eternal Spring Shrine]] * [[The common end of myriad good deeds]] * [[:en:Greek hero cult|Greek hero cult]] == Notes == {{Notelist}} == References == {{Reflist}} == External Links == * [http://www.okinawa-gokoku.jp/ Official website {{draft}} {{Promising draft|reason=the high amount of work put into it over a long time, see edit history}} {{afc comment|1=This page was originally created by Immanuelle as a wikipedia draft. It was then automatically transferred to everybodywiki and recovered from there. In the future it will be good to import the revisions. Check page history in case this page was moved, since the everybodywiki page may have been under a different title[[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 20:10, 11 February 2025 (UTC)}} {{draft}} {{Infobox religious building| religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | image = Okinawaken Gokoku Shrine haiden.jpg | map_type = Japan | coordinates = | map_size = | map_alt = | map_relief = | map_caption = | location = | festival = | consecration_year = 1936 | type = ''[[Gokoku shrine]]''
(Formerly ''[[Shokonsha]]'') | leadership = | website = | architecture_style = | founded_by = | established = | date_destroyed = | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | name = Okinawa Prefecture Gokoku Shrine | image = [[File:Okinawaken Gokoku Shrine haiden.jpg|250px|Okinawa Prefecture Gokoku Shrine Haiden]]
Haiden | coordinates = {{Coord|26|12|11.6|N|127|40|33.6|E|scale:25000_region:JP-47_type:landmark|display=inline,title}} | location = 奥武山町44, Naha City, Okinawa Prefecture, Japan | deity = * Local war dead enshrined at {{ill|Yasukuni Shrine|ja|靖国神社}} * War dead from mainland Japan who perished during the {{ill|Battle of Okinawa|ja|沖縄戦}} * Civilian victims and student casualties of the war | established = 1936 (Shōwa 11) | festivals = April 23, October 23 }} '''Okinawa Prefecture Gokoku Shrine''' ({{nihongo|沖縄県護国神社|Okinawaken Gokoku Jinja}}) is a [[Shinto shrine]] located within {{ill|Onoyama Park|ja|奥武山公園}}, {{ill|Naha|ja|那覇市}}, Okinawa Prefecture, Japan. The shrine is an independent religious institution not affiliated with [[Jinja Honcho]]. {{Interlanguage link|{{PAGENAME}}|ja|沖縄県護国神社}} is a [[Shinto shrine]] located in [[Japan]]. It is a [[Gokoku Shrines|Gokoku Shrine]], or a shrine dedicated to war dead.{{Efn|Is this [[WP:BLUE]] considering that the shrine is literally named as such?}} Such shrines were made to serve to enshrine the war dead, and they were all considered "branches" of [[Yasukuni Shrine]]. They were renamed from [[Shōkonsha|Shokonsha]] in 1939.{{Cite journal |last=TAKAYAMA |first=K. PETER |date=1990 |title=Enshrinement and Persistency of Japanese Religion |url=https://www.jstor.org/stable/23917081 |journal=Journal of Church and State |volume=32 |issue=3 |pages=527–547 |issn=0021-969X}} The Okinawa Gokoku-jinja Shrine also enshrines the dead of the [[Battle of Okinawa]], including ordinary residents, schoolchildren in distress, and civilian war dead.[[護国神社#山中|山中 2013]]、212頁{{Cite web |date=2022-03-08 |title=沖縄県護国神社について｜初詣、ご祈願、地鎮祭は、沖縄県護国神社 |url=https://www.okinawa-gokoku.jp/about.php |url-status=dead |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20220308190702/https://www.okinawa-gokoku.jp/about.php |archive-date=8 March 2022 |access-date=2022-03-08 |website=}} In addition, about 10,000 mobilized students and female volunteer corps members who were victims of the [[Atomic bomb]] are also enshrined as deities at [[Hiroshima Gokoku Shrine]].[[護国神社#山中|山中 2013]]、154頁{{Cite web |date=2021-01-20 |title=由緒 {{!}} 広島護國神社 |url=https://www.h-gokoku.or.jp/yuisho/ |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20210120233005/https://www.h-gokoku.or.jp/yuisho/ |archive-date=2021-01-20 |access-date=2022-03-08}} The main hall burned down during the [[Pacific War]] and was reconstructed in 1959.{{Cite book |last=Takenaka |first=Akiko |url=http://dx.doi.org/10.1515/9780824856939 |title=Yasukuni Shrine |date=2017-10-16 |publisher=University of Hawaii Press |isbn=978-0-8248-5693-9 |pages=158}} == Enshrined Deities == The shrine enshrines spirits of war dead from Okinawa Prefecture and mainland Japan, including civilians and student casualties who perished during the {{ill|Battle of Okinawa|ja|沖縄戦}}. As of October 2005, the shrine enshrines 178,689 spirits, the highest number among {{ill|Gokoku shrines|ja|護国神社}} nationwide. == History == [[File:Okinawaken_Gogoku_Shrine_torii.jpeg|thumb|right|170px|Torii gate]] The shrine traces its origins to a {{ill|Shōkonsha|ja|招魂社}} established in 1936 to honor those who died in service to Japan during conflicts such as the {{ill|First Sino-Japanese War|ja|日清戦争}} and the {{ill|Russo-Japanese War|ja|日露戦争}}.加治順人『沖縄の神社』p115　ひるぎ社、2000年10月30日 In 1940, it was designated as a Gokoku Shrine by the Ministry of Home Affairs and renamed "Okinawa Prefecture Gokoku Shrine."『全國護國神社會五十年史』（1997年3月1日、全國護國神社會発行）287頁。 The shrine was destroyed during the {{ill|Battle of Okinawa|ja|沖縄戦}} in April 1945. A temporary shrine was constructed on April 26, 1959. In the same year, relics of spirits from the {{ill|Yasukuni Shrine|ja|靖国神社}} were transferred to the shrine, honoring mainland Japanese who died in the Battle of Okinawa. The current main shrine building was completed in October 1965, with the relocation ceremony held on November 19 and the restoration commemorative festival on November 20.加治順人『沖縄の神社』p143　ひるぎ社、2000年10月30日 In 1980, a Kaguraden (dance hall) was added to the grounds.{{DEFAULTSORT:Okinawa Prefecture Gokoku Shrine}} [[Category:Shinto shrines in Okinawa Prefecture]] [[Category:Naha]] [[Category:Gokoku shrines]] [[Category:Independent Shinto shrines]] [[Category:Ken-sha]] [[Category:Buildings and structures in Naha]] [[Category:History of Okinawa Prefecture]] == See Also == * [[Controversies surrounding Yasukuni Shrine]] * [[Martyrs' shrines (China)|Martyr's Shrines]] * [[:en:Martial temple|Martial temple]] and [[:en:Wen Wu temple|Wen Wu temple]] * [[National Revolutionary Martyrs' Shrine]] * [[Eternal Spring Shrine]] * [[:en:Chinese Cultural Renaissance|Chinese Cultural Renaissance]] * [[Ancestral shrine]] * [[:en:Gallant Garden|Gallant Garden]] * [[Gokoku Shrines]] * [[:en:Tomb of the Unknown Soldier|Tomb of the Unknown Soldier]] * [[:en:Arlington National Cemetery|Arlington National Cemetery]] * [[:en:Valhalla|Valhalla]] (home to the souls of fallen warriors in Scandinavian mythology) * [[:en:Walhalla (memorial)|Walhalla Shrine]] (a hall of fame in Germany honoring "commendable and honorable Germans") * [[Eternal Spring Shrine]] * [[The common end of myriad good deeds]] * [[:en:Greek hero cult|Greek hero cult]] == Notes == {{Notelist}} == References == {{Reflist}} == External Links == * [http://www.okinawa-gokoku.jp/ Official website of Okinawa Prefecture Gokoku Shrine] {{Shinto Horizontal}} {{Shinto-stub}} {{DEFAULTSORT:Okinawa Prefecture Gokoku Shrine}} [[Category:Shinto shrines in Okinawa Prefecture]] [[Category:Naha]] [[Category:Gokoku shrines]] [[Category:Independent Shinto shrines]] [[Category:Ken-sha]] [[Category:Buildings and structures in Naha]] [[Category:History of Okinawa Prefecture]] {{Draft categories| [[Category:Gokoku shrines]] [[Category:1875 establishments]] [[Category:Shinto shrines in Okinawa Prefecture]] [[Category:Okinawa Gokoku Shrine]][[Category:Unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:Unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:Okinawa Gokoku Shrine]][[Category:Okinawa Gokoku Shrine]][[Category:Unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:Okinawa Gokoku Shrine]][[Category:bump1-19]] [[Category:refreshing categories imported on 2024-01-19]] [[Category:refreshing imported pages]] [[Category:refreshing redirects 1-26]] [[Category:second attempt at refreshing on January 27 2024]][[Category:Okinawa Gokoku Shrine]][[Category:second attempt at refreshing on January 27 2024]] [[Category:second attempt at refreshing on January 27 2024]][[Category:Okinawa Gokoku Shrine]][[Category:second attempt at refreshing on January 27 2024]] [[Category:from everydodywiki with needed marking of original title]] [[ja:沖縄県護国神社]] }} {{⚠️🚨COPIED from EverybodyWiki ❗❕⚠️😡😤Please respect Licence CC-BY-SA ❗}} {{Source Wikipedia}}

revision.text:
{{draft}} {{Promising draft|reason=the high amount of work put into it over a long time, see edit history}} {{afc comment|1=This page was originally created by Immanuelle as a wikipedia draft. It was then automatically transferred to everybodywiki and recovered from there. In the future it will be good to import the revisions. Check page history in case this page was moved, since the everybodywiki page may have been under a different title[[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 20:10, 11 February 2025 (UTC)}} {{draft}} {{Infobox religious building| religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | image = Okinawaken Gokoku Shrine haiden.jpg | map_type = Japan | coordinates = | map_size = | map_alt = | map_relief = | map_caption = | location = | festival = | consecration_year = 1936 | type = ''[[Gokoku shrine]]''
(Formerly ''[[Shokonsha]]'') | leadership = | website = | architecture_style = | founded_by = | established = | date_destroyed = | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | name = Okinawa Prefecture Gokoku Shrine | image = [[File:Okinawaken Gokoku Shrine haiden.jpg|250px|Okinawa Prefecture Gokoku Shrine Haiden]]
Haiden | coordinates = {{Coord|26|12|11.6|N|127|40|33.6|E|scale:25000_region:JP-47_type:landmark|display=inline,title}} | location = 奥武山町44, Naha City, Okinawa Prefecture, Japan | deity = * Local war dead enshrined at {{ill|Yasukuni Shrine|ja|靖国神社}} * War dead from mainland Japan who perished during the {{ill|Battle of Okinawa|ja|沖縄戦}} * Civilian victims and student casualties of the war | established = 1936 (Shōwa 11) | festivals = April 23, October 23 }} '''Okinawa Prefecture Gokoku Shrine''' ({{nihongo|沖縄県護国神社|Okinawaken Gokoku Jinja}}) is a [[Shinto shrine]] located within {{ill|Onoyama Park|ja|奥武山公園}}, {{ill|Naha|ja|那覇市}}, Okinawa Prefecture, Japan. The shrine is an independent religious institution not affiliated with [[Jinja Honcho]]. {{Interlanguage link|{{PAGENAME}}|ja|沖縄県護国神社}} is a [[Shinto shrine]] located in [[Japan]]. It is a [[Gokoku Shrines|Gokoku Shrine]], or a shrine dedicated to war dead.{{Efn|Is this [[WP:BLUE]] considering that the shrine is literally named as such?}} Such shrines were made to serve to enshrine the war dead, and they were all considered "branches" of [[Yasukuni Shrine]]. They were renamed from [[Shōkonsha|Shokonsha]] in 1939.{{Cite journal |last=TAKAYAMA |first=K. PETER |date=1990 |title=Enshrinement and Persistency of Japanese Religion |url=https://www.jstor.org/stable/23917081 |journal=Journal of Church and State |volume=32 |issue=3 |pages=527–547 |issn=0021-969X}} The Okinawa Gokoku-jinja Shrine also enshrines the dead of the [[Battle of Okinawa]], including ordinary residents, schoolchildren in distress, and civilian war dead.[[護国神社#山中|山中 2013]]、212頁{{Cite web |date=2022-03-08 |title=沖縄県護国神社について｜初詣、ご祈願、地鎮祭は、沖縄県護国神社 |url=https://www.okinawa-gokoku.jp/about.php |url-status=dead |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20220308190702/https://www.okinawa-gokoku.jp/about.php |archive-date=8 March 2022 |access-date=2022-03-08 |website=}} In addition, about 10,000 mobilized students and female volunteer corps members who were victims of the [[Atomic bomb]] are also enshrined as deities at [[Hiroshima Gokoku Shrine]].[[護国神社#山中|山中 2013]]、154頁{{Cite web |date=2021-01-20 |title=由緒 {{!}} 広島護國神社 |url=https://www.h-gokoku.or.jp/yuisho/ |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20210120233005/https://www.h-gokoku.or.jp/yuisho/ |archive-date=2021-01-20 |access-date=2022-03-08}} The main hall burned down during the [[Pacific War]] and was reconstructed in 1959.{{Cite book |last=Takenaka |first=Akiko |url=http://dx.doi.org/10.1515/9780824856939 |title=Yasukuni Shrine |date=2017-10-16 |publisher=University of Hawaii Press |isbn=978-0-8248-5693-9 |pages=158}} == Enshrined Deities == The shrine enshrines spirits of war dead from Okinawa Prefecture and mainland Japan, including civilians and student casualties who perished during the {{ill|Battle of Okinawa|ja|沖縄戦}}. As of October 2005, the shrine enshrines 178,689 spirits, the highest number among {{ill|Gokoku shrines|ja|護国神社}} nationwide. == History == [[File:Okinawaken_Gogoku_Shrine_torii.jpeg|thumb|right|170px|Torii gate]] The shrine traces its origins to a {{ill|Shōkonsha|ja|招魂社}} established in 1936 to honor those who died in service to Japan during conflicts such as the {{ill|First Sino-Japanese War|ja|日清戦争}} and the {{ill|Russo-Japanese War|ja|日露戦争}}.加治順人『沖縄の神社』p115　ひるぎ社、2000年10月30日 In 1940, it was designated as a Gokoku Shrine by the Ministry of Home Affairs and renamed "Okinawa Prefecture Gokoku Shrine."『全國護國神社會五十年史』（1997年3月1日、全國護國神社會発行）287頁。 The shrine was destroyed during the {{ill|Battle of Okinawa|ja|沖縄戦}} in April 1945. A temporary shrine was constructed on April 26, 1959. In the same year, relics of spirits from the {{ill|Yasukuni Shrine|ja|靖国神社}} were transferred to the shrine, honoring mainland Japanese who died in the Battle of Okinawa. The current main shrine building was completed in October 1965, with the relocation ceremony held on November 19 and the restoration commemorative festival on November 20.加治順人『沖縄の神社』p143　ひるぎ社、2000年10月30日 In 1980, a Kaguraden (dance hall) was added to the grounds.{{DEFAULTSORT:Okinawa Prefecture Gokoku Shrine}} [[Category:Shinto shrines in Okinawa Prefecture]] [[Category:Naha]] [[Category:Gokoku shrines]] [[Category:Independent Shinto shrines]] [[Category:Ken-sha]] [[Category:Buildings and structures in Naha]] [[Category:History of Okinawa Prefecture]] == See Also == * [[Controversies surrounding Yasukuni Shrine]] * [[Martyrs' shrines (China)|Martyr's Shrines]] * [[:en:Martial temple|Martial temple]] and [[:en:Wen Wu temple|Wen Wu temple]] * [[National Revolutionary Martyrs' Shrine]] * [[Eternal Spring Shrine]] * [[:en:Chinese Cultural Renaissance|Chinese Cultural Renaissance]] * [[Ancestral shrine]] * [[:en:Gallant Garden|Gallant Garden]] * [[Gokoku Shrines]] * [[:en:Tomb of the Unknown Soldier|Tomb of the Unknown Soldier]] * [[:en:Arlington National Cemetery|Arlington National Cemetery]] * [[:en:Valhalla|Valhalla]] (home to the souls of fallen warriors in Scandinavian mythology) * [[:en:Walhalla (memorial)|Walhalla Shrine]] (a hall of fame in Germany honoring "commendable and honorable Germans") * [[Eternal Spring Shrine]] * [[The common end of myriad good deeds]] * [[:en:Greek hero cult|Greek hero cult]] == Notes == {{Notelist}} == References == {{Reflist}} == External Links == * [http://www.okinawa-gokoku.jp/ Official website of Okinawa Prefecture Gokoku Shrine] {{Shinto Horizontal}} {{Shinto-stub}} {{DEFAULTSORT:Okinawa Prefecture Gokoku Shrine}} [[Category:Shinto shrines in Okinawa Prefecture]] [[Category:Naha]] [[Category:Gokoku shrines]] [[Category:Independent Shinto shrines]] [[Category:Ken-sha]] [[Category:Buildings and structures in Naha]] [[Category:History of Okinawa Prefecture]] {{Draft categories| [[Category:Gokoku shrines]] [[Category:1875 establishments]] [[Category:Shinto shrines in Okinawa Prefecture]] [[Category:Okinawa Gokoku Shrine]][[Category:Unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:Unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:Okinawa Gokoku Shrine]][[Category:Okinawa Gokoku Shrine]][[Category:Unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:Okinawa Gokoku Shrine]][[Category:bump1-19]] [[Category:refreshing categories imported on 2024-01-19]] [[Category:refreshing imported pages]] [[Category:refreshing redirects 1-26]] [[Category:second attempt at refreshing on January 27 2024]][[Category:Okinawa Gokoku Shrine]][[Category:second attempt at refreshing on January 27 2024]] [[Category:second attempt at refreshing on January 27 2024]][[Category:Okinawa Gokoku Shrine]][[Category:second attempt at refreshing on January 27 2024]] [[Category:Pages from everybodywiki under right title]] [[ja:沖縄県護国神社]] }} {{⚠️🚨COPIED from EverybodyWiki ❗❕⚠️😡😤Please respect Licence CC-BY-SA ❗}} {{Source Wikipedia}}
