revision.text:
[[co:commons:Category:Okinawaken-Gokoku-jinja]][[ja:沖縄県護国神社]] [[co:commons:Category:Okinawaken-Gokoku-jinja]][[ja:沖縄県護国神社]] [[sn:commons:Category:Okinawaken-Gokoku-jinja]][[ja:沖縄県護国神社]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q11552847]] Wikidata name: Okinawa Gokoku Shrine [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 08:45, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{afc comment|1=This page was originally created in the draft namespace on english wikipedia. It was then deleted but preserved on everybodywiki. The imported history from everybodywiki is patchwork and contains all the contributing users, but not all the revisions are actually present. Lets hope this is sufficient attribution though especially since most of them are mine.[[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 08:28, 2 May 2025 (UTC)}} {{afc comment|1=see for the history[[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 02:25, 31 March 2025 (UTC)}} {{afc comment|1=Page originally made on english wikipedia in draftspace. g13 deleted and archived on everybodywiki. Go to {{ill|Wikipedia:Requests for undeletion|en|Wikipedia:Requests for undeletion|WD=Q13429672|lt=Wikipedia:Requests for undeletion}} to request undeletion of original page [[:en:draft:Okinawa Gokoku Shrine]] and import the revisions to here for attribution. View on everybodywiki here [[:every:Okinawa Gokoku Shrine|Okinawa Gokoku Shrine]]. [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 21:01, 16 February 2025 (UTC)}} {{afc comment|1=This page was originally created by Immanuelle as a wikipedia draft. It was then automatically transferred to everybodywiki and recovered from there. In the future it will be good to import the revisions. Check page history in case this page was moved, since the everybodywiki page may have been under a different title[[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 20:10, 11 February 2025 (UTC)}} {{Infobox religious building| religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | image = Okinawaken Gokoku Shrine haiden.jpg | map_type = Japan | coordinates = | map_size = | map_alt = | map_relief = | map_caption = | location = | festival = | consecration_year = 1936 | type = ''[[Gokoku Shrines|Gokoku shrine]]''
(Formerly ''[[Gokoku Shrines|Shokonsha]]'') | leadership = | website = | architecture_style = | founded_by = | established = | date_destroyed = | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | name = Okinawa Prefecture Gokoku Shrine | image = [[File:Okinawaken Gokoku Shrine haiden.jpg|250px|Okinawa Prefecture Gokoku Shrine Haiden]]
Haiden | coordinates = {{Coord|26|12|11.6|N|127|40|33.6|E|scale:25000_region:JP-47_type:landmark|display=inline,title}} | location = 奥武山町44, Naha City, Okinawa Prefecture, Japan | deity = * Local war dead enshrined at {{ill|Yasukuni Shrine|en|Yasukuni Shrine|WD=Q242803|lt=Yasukuni Shrine}} * War dead from mainland Japan who perished during the {{ill|Battle of Okinawa|en|Battle of Okinawa|WD=Q192660|lt=Battle of Okinawa}} * Civilian victims and student casualties of the war | established = 1936 (Shōwa 11) | festivals = April 23, October 23 }} '''Okinawa Prefecture Gokoku Shrine''' ({{nihongo|沖縄県護国神社|1=Okinawa Gokoku Shrine|Okinawaken Gokoku Jinja}}) is a {{ill|Shinto shrine|en|Shinto shrine|WD=Q845945|lt=Shinto shrine}} located within {{ill|Onoyama Park|ja|奥武山公園|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Onoyama Park|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 沖縄県営奥武山公園}}, {{ill|Naha|en|Naha|WD=Q181966|lt=Naha}}, Okinawa Prefecture, Japan. The shrine is an independent religious institution not affiliated with [[:en:Jinja Honcho|Jinja Honcho]]. {{ill|Okinawa Gokoku Shrine|ja|沖縄県護国神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Okinawa Gokoku Shrine|qq=}} is a {{ill|Shinto shrine|en|Shinto shrine|WD=Q845945|lt=Shinto shrine}} located in {{ill|Japan|en|Japan|WD=Q17|lt=Japan}}. It is a [[Gokoku Shrines|Gokoku Shrine]], or a shrine dedicated to war dead.{{Efn|Is this [[WP:BLUE]] considering that the shrine is literally named as such?}} Such shrines were made to serve to enshrine the war dead, and they were all considered "branches" of {{ill|Yasukuni Shrine|en|Yasukuni Shrine|WD=Q242803|lt=Yasukuni Shrine}}. They were renamed from {{ill|Shōkonsha|en|Shōkonsha|WD=Q11497810|lt=Shokonsha}} in 1939.{{Cite journal |last=TAKAYAMA |first=K. PETER |date=1990 |title=Enshrinement and Persistency of Japanese Religion |url=https://www.jstor.org/stable/23917081 |journal=Journal of Church and State |volume=32 |issue=3 |pages=527–547 |issn=0021-969X}} The Okinawa Gokoku-jinja Shrine also enshrines the dead of the {{ill|Battle of Okinawa|en|Battle of Okinawa|WD=Q192660|lt=Battle of Okinawa}}, including ordinary residents, schoolchildren in distress, and civilian war dead.[[護国神社#山中|山中 2013]]、212頁{{Cite web |date=2022-03-08 |title=沖縄県護国神社について｜初詣、ご祈願、地鎮祭は、沖縄県護国神社 |url=https://www.okinawa-gokoku.jp/about.php |url-status=dead |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20220308190702/https://www.okinawa-gokoku.jp/about.php |archive-date=8 March 2022 |access-date=2022-03-08 |website=}} In addition, about 10,000 mobilized students and female volunteer corps members who were victims of the {{ill|Atomic bomb|en|Atomic bomb|WD=Q650051|lt=Atomic bomb}} are also enshrined as deities at [[Hiroshima Gokoku Shrine]].[[護国神社#山中|山中 2013]]、154頁{{Cite web |date=2021-01-20 |title=由緒 {{!}} 広島護國神社 |url=https://www.h-gokoku.or.jp/yuisho/ |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20210120233005/https://www.h-gokoku.or.jp/yuisho/ |archive-date=2021-01-20 |access-date=2022-03-08}} The main hall burned down during the {{ill|Pacific War|en|Pacific War|WD=Q184425|lt=Pacific War}} and was reconstructed in 1959.{{Cite book |last=Takenaka |first=Akiko |url=http://dx.doi.org/10.1515/9780824856939 |title=Yasukuni Shrine |date=2017-10-16 |publisher=University of Hawaii Press |isbn=978-0-8248-5693-9 |pages=158}} == Enshrined Deities == The shrine enshrines spirits of war dead from Okinawa Prefecture and mainland Japan, including civilians and student casualties who perished during the {{ill|Battle of Okinawa|en|Battle of Okinawa|WD=Q192660|lt=Battle of Okinawa}}. As of October 2005, the shrine enshrines 178,689 spirits, the highest number among {{ill|Gokoku shrines|en|Gokoku shrines|WD=Q1534477|lt=Gokoku shrines}} nationwide. == History == [[File:Okinawaken_Gogoku_Shrine_torii.jpeg|thumb|right|170px|Torii gate]] The shrine traces its origins to a {{ill|Shōkonsha|ja|招魂社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shōkonsha|comment=changed title based on wikidata|1=Shokonsha|WD=Q11497810}} established in 1936 to honor those who died in service to Japan during conflicts such as the {{ill|First Sino-Japanese War|en|First Sino-Japanese War|WD=Q178687|lt=First Sino-Japanese War}} and the {{ill|Russo-Japanese War|en|Russo-Japanese War|WD=Q159950|lt=Russo-Japanese War}}.加治順人『沖縄の神社』p115　ひるぎ社、2000th year 10月30日 In 1940, it was designated as a Gokoku Shrine by the Ministry of Home Affairs and renamed "Okinawa Prefecture Gokoku Shrine."『全國護國神社會五十年史』（1997th year 3月1日、全國護國神社會発行）287頁。 The shrine was destroyed during the {{ill|Battle of Okinawa|en|Battle of Okinawa|WD=Q192660|lt=Battle of Okinawa}} in April 1945. A temporary shrine was constructed on April 26, 1959. In the same year, relics of spirits from the {{ill|Yasukuni Shrine|en|Yasukuni Shrine|WD=Q242803|lt=Yasukuni Shrine}} were transferred to the shrine, honoring mainland Japanese who died in the Battle of Okinawa. The current main shrine building was completed in October 1965, with the relocation ceremony held on November 19 and the restoration commemorative festival on November 20.加治順人『沖縄の神社』p143　ひるぎ社、2000th year 10月30日 In 1980, a Kaguraden (dance hall) was added to the grounds. == See Also == * [[Controversies surrounding Yasukuni Shrine]] * {{ill|Martyrs' shrines (China)|en|Martyrs' shrines (China)|WD=Q6800982|lt=Martyr's Shrines}} * {{ill|Martial temple|en|Martial temple|WD=Q11124210|lt=Martial temple}} and {{ill|Wen Wu temple|en|Wen Wu temple|WD=Q7231588|lt=Wen Wu temple}} * {{ill|National Revolutionary Martyrs' Shrine|en|National Revolutionary Martyrs' Shrine|WD=Q5956488|lt=National Revolutionary Martyrs' Shrine}} * {{ill|Eternal Spring Shrine|en|Eternal Spring Shrine|WD=Q4501089|lt=Eternal Spring Shrine}} * {{ill|Chinese Cultural Renaissance|en|Chinese Cultural Renaissance|WD=Q1073969|lt=Chinese Cultural Renaissance}} * [[Ancestral shrine]] * {{ill|Gallant Garden|en|Gallant Garden|WD=Q5518867|lt=Gallant Garden}} * [[Gokoku Shrines]] * {{ill|Tomb of the Unknown Soldier|en|Tomb of the Unknown Soldier|WD=Q1541043|lt=Tomb of the Unknown Soldier}} * {{ill|Arlington National Cemetery|en|Arlington National Cemetery|WD=Q216344|lt=Arlington National Cemetery}} * {{ill|Valhalla|en|Valhalla|WD=Q177860|lt=Valhalla}} (home to the souls of fallen warriors in Scandinavian mythology) * {{ill|Walhalla (memorial)|en|Walhalla (memorial)|WD=Q321240|lt=Walhalla Shrine}} (a hall of fame in Germany honoring "commendable and honorable Germans") * {{ill|Eternal Spring Shrine|en|Eternal Spring Shrine|WD=Q4501089|lt=Eternal Spring Shrine}} * {{ill|The common end of myriad good deeds|en|The common end of myriad good deeds|WD=Q9609095|lt=The common end of myriad good deeds}} * {{ill|Greek hero cult|en|Greek hero cult|WD=Q1878906|lt=Greek hero cult}} == Notes == {{Notelist}} == References == {{Reflist}} == External Links == * [http://www.okinawa-gokoku.jp/ Official website of Okinawa Prefecture Gokoku Shrine] {{Shinto Horizontal}} {{Shinto-stub}} }} {{Okinawan Shrines}} \ {{⚠️🚨COPIED from EverybodyWiki ❗❕⚠️😡😤Please respect Licence CC-BY-SA ❗}} [[co:commons:Category:Okinawaken-Gokoku-jinja]][[ja:沖縄県護国神社]] [[co:commons:Category:Okinawaken-Gokoku-jinja]][[ja:沖縄県護国神社]] [[sn:commons:Category:Okinawaken-Gokoku-jinja]][[ja:沖縄県護国神社]] to request undeletion of original page [[:en:draft:Okinawa Gokoku Shrine]] and import the revisions to here for attribution. View on everybodywiki here [[:every:Okinawa Gokoku Shrine|Okinawa Gokoku Shrine]]. [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 21:01, 16 February 2025 (UTC)}} {{Infobox religious building| religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | image = Okinawaken Gokoku Shrine haiden.jpg | map_type = Japan | coordinates = | map_size = | map_alt = | map_relief = | map_caption = | location = | festival = | consecration_year = 1936 | type = ''[[Gokoku Shrines|Gokoku shrine]]''
(Formerly ''[[Gokoku Shrines|Shokonsha]]'') | leadership = | website = | architecture_style = | founded_by = | established = | date_destroyed = | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | name = Okinawa Prefecture Gokoku Shrine | image = [[File:Okinawaken Gokoku Shrine haiden.jpg|250px|Okinawa Prefecture Gokoku Shrine Haiden]]
Haiden | coordinates = {{Coord|26|12|11.6|N|127|40|33.6|E|scale:25000_region:JP-47_type:landmark|display=inline,title}} | location = 奥武山町44, Naha City, Okinawa Prefecture, Japan | deity = * Local war dead enshrined at {{ill|Yasukuni Shrine|en|Yasukuni Shrine|WD=Q242803|lt=Yasukuni Shrine}} * War dead from mainland Japan who perished during the {{ill|Battle of Okinawa|en|Battle of Okinawa|WD=Q192660|lt=Battle of Okinawa}} * Civilian victims and student casualties of the war | established = 1936 (Shōwa 11) | festivals = April 23, October 23 }} '''Okinawa Prefecture Gokoku Shrine''' ({{nihongo|沖縄県護国神社|1=Okinawa Gokoku Shrine|Okinawaken Gokoku Jinja}}) is a {{ill|Shinto shrine|en|Shinto shrine|WD=Q845945|lt=Shinto shrine}} located within {{ill|Onoyama Park|ja|奥武山公園|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Onoyama Park|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 沖縄県営奥武山公園}}, {{ill|Naha|en|Naha|WD=Q181966|lt=Naha}}, Okinawa Prefecture, Japan. The shrine is an independent religious institution not affiliated with [[:en:Jinja Honcho|Jinja Honcho]]. {{ill|Okinawa Gokoku Shrine|ja|沖縄県護国神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Okinawa Gokoku Shrine|qq=}} is a {{ill|Shinto shrine|en|Shinto shrine|WD=Q845945|lt=Shinto shrine}} located in {{ill|Japan|en|Japan|WD=Q17|lt=Japan}}. It is a [[Gokoku Shrines|Gokoku Shrine]], or a shrine dedicated to war dead.{{Efn|Is this [[WP:BLUE]] considering that the shrine is literally named as such?}} Such shrines were made to serve to enshrine the war dead, and they were all considered "branches" of {{ill|Yasukuni Shrine|en|Yasukuni Shrine|WD=Q242803|lt=Yasukuni Shrine}}. They were renamed from {{ill|Shōkonsha|en|Shōkonsha|WD=Q11497810|lt=Shokonsha}} in 1939.{{Cite journal |last=TAKAYAMA |first=K. PETER |date=1990 |title=Enshrinement and Persistency of Japanese Religion |url=https://www.jstor.org/stable/23917081 |journal=Journal of Church and State |volume=32 |issue=3 |pages=527–547 |issn=0021-969X}} The Okinawa Gokoku-jinja Shrine also enshrines the dead of the {{ill|Battle of Okinawa|en|Battle of Okinawa|WD=Q192660|lt=Battle of Okinawa}}, including ordinary residents, schoolchildren in distress, and civilian war dead.[[護国神社#山中|山中 2013]]、212頁{{Cite web |date=2022-03-08 |title=沖縄県護国神社について｜初詣、ご祈願、地鎮祭は、沖縄県護国神社 |url=https://www.okinawa-gokoku.jp/about.php |url-status=dead |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20220308190702/https://www.okinawa-gokoku.jp/about.php |archive-date=8 March 2022 |access-date=2022-03-08 |website=}} In addition, about 10,000 mobilized students and female volunteer corps members who were victims of the {{ill|Atomic bomb|en|Atomic bomb|WD=Q650051|lt=Atomic bomb}} are also enshrined as deities at [[Hiroshima Gokoku Shrine]].[[護国神社#山中|山中 2013]]、154頁{{Cite web |date=2021-01-20 |title=由緒 {{!}} 広島護國神社 |url=https://www.h-gokoku.or.jp/yuisho/ |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20210120233005/https://www.h-gokoku.or.jp/yuisho/ |archive-date=2021-01-20 |access-date=2022-03-08}} The main hall burned down during the {{ill|Pacific War|en|Pacific War|WD=Q184425|lt=Pacific War}} and was reconstructed in 1959.{{Cite book |last=Takenaka |first=Akiko |url=http://dx.doi.org/10.1515/9780824856939 |title=Yasukuni Shrine |date=2017-10-16 |publisher=University of Hawaii Press |isbn=978-0-8248-5693-9 |pages=158}} == Enshrined Deities == The shrine enshrines spirits of war dead from Okinawa Prefecture and mainland Japan, including civilians and student casualties who perished during the {{ill|Battle of Okinawa|en|Battle of Okinawa|WD=Q192660|lt=Battle of Okinawa}}. As of October 2005, the shrine enshrines 178,689 spirits, the highest number among {{ill|Gokoku shrines|en|Gokoku shrines|WD=Q1534477|lt=Gokoku shrines}} nationwide. == History == [[File:Okinawaken_Gogoku_Shrine_torii.jpeg|thumb|right|170px|Torii gate]] The shrine traces its origins to a {{ill|Shōkonsha|ja|招魂社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shōkonsha|comment=changed title based on wikidata|1=Shokonsha|WD=Q11497810}} established in 1936 to honor those who died in service to Japan during conflicts such as the {{ill|First Sino-Japanese War|en|First Sino-Japanese War|WD=Q178687|lt=First Sino-Japanese War}} and the {{ill|Russo-Japanese War|en|Russo-Japanese War|WD=Q159950|lt=Russo-Japanese War}}.加治順人『沖縄の神社』p115　ひるぎ社、2000th year 10月30日 In 1940, it was designated as a Gokoku Shrine by the Ministry of Home Affairs and renamed "Okinawa Prefecture Gokoku Shrine."『全國護國神社會五十年史』（1997th year 3月1日、全國護國神社會発行）287頁。 The shrine was destroyed during the {{ill|Battle of Okinawa|en|Battle of Okinawa|WD=Q192660|lt=Battle of Okinawa}} in April 1945. A temporary shrine was constructed on April 26, 1959. In the same year, relics of spirits from the {{ill|Yasukuni Shrine|en|Yasukuni Shrine|WD=Q242803|lt=Yasukuni Shrine}} were transferred to the shrine, honoring mainland Japanese who died in the Battle of Okinawa. The current main shrine building was completed in October 1965, with the relocation ceremony held on November 19 and the restoration commemorative festival on November 20.加治順人『沖縄の神社』p143　ひるぎ社、2000th year 10月30日 In 1980, a Kaguraden (dance hall) was added to the grounds. == See Also == * [[Controversies surrounding Yasukuni Shrine]] * {{ill|Martyrs' shrines (China)|en|Martyrs' shrines (China)|WD=Q6800982|lt=Martyr's Shrines}} * {{ill|Martial temple|en|Martial temple|WD=Q11124210|lt=Martial temple}} and {{ill|Wen Wu temple|en|Wen Wu temple|WD=Q7231588|lt=Wen Wu temple}} * {{ill|National Revolutionary Martyrs' Shrine|en|National Revolutionary Martyrs' Shrine|WD=Q5956488|lt=National Revolutionary Martyrs' Shrine}} * {{ill|Eternal Spring Shrine|en|Eternal Spring Shrine|WD=Q4501089|lt=Eternal Spring Shrine}} * {{ill|Chinese Cultural Renaissance|en|Chinese Cultural Renaissance|WD=Q1073969|lt=Chinese Cultural Renaissance}} * [[Ancestral shrine]] * {{ill|Gallant Garden|en|Gallant Garden|WD=Q5518867|lt=Gallant Garden}} * [[Gokoku Shrines]] * {{ill|Tomb of the Unknown Soldier|en|Tomb of the Unknown Soldier|WD=Q1541043|lt=Tomb of the Unknown Soldier}} * {{ill|Arlington National Cemetery|en|Arlington National Cemetery|WD=Q216344|lt=Arlington National Cemetery}} * {{ill|Valhalla|en|Valhalla|WD=Q177860|lt=Valhalla}} (home to the souls of fallen warriors in Scandinavian mythology) * {{ill|Walhalla (memorial)|en|Walhalla (memorial)|WD=Q321240|lt=Walhalla Shrine}} (a hall of fame in Germany honoring "commendable and honorable Germans") * {{ill|Eternal Spring Shrine|en|Eternal Spring Shrine|WD=Q4501089|lt=Eternal Spring Shrine}} * {{ill|The common end of myriad good deeds|en|The common end of myriad good deeds|WD=Q9609095|lt=The common end of myriad good deeds}} * {{ill|Greek hero cult|en|Greek hero cult|WD=Q1878906|lt=Greek hero cult}} == Notes == {{Notelist}} == References == {{Reflist}} == External Links == * [http://www.okinawa-gokoku.jp/ It is a [[Gokoku Shrines|Gokoku Shrine]], or a shrine dedicated to war dead.{{Efn|Is this [[WP:BLUE]] considering that the shrine is literally named as such?}} Such shrines were made to serve to enshrine the war dead, and they were all considered "branches" of {{ill|Yasukuni Shrine|en|Yasukuni Shrine|WD=Q242803|lt=Yasukuni Shrine}}. They were renamed from {{ill|Shōkonsha|en|Shōkonsha|WD=Q11497810|lt=Shokonsha}} in 1939.{{Cite journal |last=TAKAYAMA |first=K. PETER |date=1990 |title=Enshrinement and Persistency of Japanese Religion |url=https://www.jstor.org/stable/23917081 |journal=Journal of Church and State |volume=32 |issue=3 |pages=527–547 |issn=0021-969X}} The Okinawa Gokoku-jinja Shrine also enshrines the dead of the {{ill|Battle of Okinawa|en|Battle of Okinawa|WD=Q192660|lt=Battle of Okinawa}}, including ordinary residents, schoolchildren in distress, and civilian war dead.[[護国神社#山中|山中 2013]]、212頁{{Cite web |date=2022-03-08 |title=沖縄県護国神社について｜初詣、ご祈願、地鎮祭は、沖縄県護国神社 |url=https://www.okinawa-gokoku.jp/about.php |url-status=dead |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20220308190702/https://www.okinawa-gokoku.jp/about.php |archive-date=8 March 2022 |access-date=2022-03-08 |website=}} In addition, about 10,000 mobilized students and female volunteer corps members who were victims of the {{ill|Atomic bomb|en|Atomic bomb|WD=Q650051|lt=Atomic bomb}} are also enshrined as deities at [[Hiroshima Gokoku Shrine]].[[護国神社#山中|山中 2013]]、154頁{{Cite web |date=2021-01-20 |title=由緒 {{!}} 広島護國神社 |url=https://www.h-gokoku.or.jp/yuisho/ |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20210120233005/https://www.h-gokoku.or.jp/yuisho/ |archive-date=2021-01-20 |access-date=2022-03-08}} The main hall burned down during the {{ill|Pacific War|en|Pacific War|WD=Q184425|lt=Pacific War}} and was reconstructed in 1959.{{Cite book |last=Takenaka |first=Akiko |url=http://dx.doi.org/10.1515/9780824856939 |title=Yasukuni Shrine |date=2017-10-16 |publisher=University of Hawaii Press |isbn=978-0-8248-5693-9 |pages=158}} == Enshrined Deities == The shrine enshrines spirits of war dead from Okinawa Prefecture and mainland Japan, including civilians and student casualties who perished during the {{ill|Battle of Okinawa|en|Battle of Okinawa|WD=Q192660|lt=Battle of Okinawa}}. As of October 2005, the shrine enshrines 178,689 spirits, the highest number among {{ill|Gokoku shrines|en|Gokoku shrines|WD=Q1534477|lt=Gokoku shrines}} nationwide. == History == [[File:Okinawaken_Gogoku_Shrine_torii.jpeg|thumb|right|170px|Torii gate]] The shrine traces its origins to a {{ill|Shōkonsha|ja|招魂社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shōkonsha|comment=changed title based on wikidata|1=Shokonsha|WD=Q11497810}} established in 1936 to honor those who died in service to Japan during conflicts such as the {{ill|First Sino-Japanese War|en|First Sino-Japanese War|WD=Q178687|lt=First Sino-Japanese War}} and the {{ill|Russo-Japanese War|en|Russo-Japanese War|WD=Q159950|lt=Russo-Japanese War}}.加治順人『沖縄の神社』p115　ひるぎ社、2000th year 10月30日 In 1940, it was designated as a Gokoku Shrine by the Ministry of Home Affairs and renamed "Okinawa Prefecture Gokoku Shrine."『全國護國神社會五十年史』（1997th year 3月1日、全國護國神社會発行）287頁。 The shrine was destroyed during the {{ill|Battle of Okinawa|en|Battle of Okinawa|WD=Q192660|lt=Battle of Okinawa}} in April 1945. A temporary shrine was constructed on April 26, 1959. In the same year, relics of spirits from the {{ill|Yasukuni Shrine|en|Yasukuni Shrine|WD=Q242803|lt=Yasukuni Shrine}} were transferred to the shrine, honoring mainland Japanese who died in the Battle of Okinawa. The current main shrine building was completed in October 1965, with the relocation ceremony held on November 19 and the restoration commemorative festival on November 20.加治順人『沖縄の神社』p143　ひるぎ社、2000th year 10月30日 In 1980, a Kaguraden (dance hall) was added to the grounds. == See Also == * [[Controversies surrounding Yasukuni Shrine]] * {{ill|Martyrs' shrines (China)|en|Martyrs' shrines (China)|WD=Q6800982|lt=Martyr's Shrines}} * {{ill|Martial temple|en|Martial temple|WD=Q11124210|lt=Martial temple}} and {{ill|Wen Wu temple|en|Wen Wu temple|WD=Q7231588|lt=Wen Wu temple}} * {{ill|National Revolutionary Martyrs' Shrine|en|National Revolutionary Martyrs' Shrine|WD=Q5956488|lt=National Revolutionary Martyrs' Shrine}} * {{ill|Eternal Spring Shrine|en|Eternal Spring Shrine|WD=Q4501089|lt=Eternal Spring Shrine}} * {{ill|Chinese Cultural Renaissance|en|Chinese Cultural Renaissance|WD=Q1073969|lt=Chinese Cultural Renaissance}} * [[Ancestral shrine]] * {{ill|Gallant Garden|en|Gallant Garden|WD=Q5518867|lt=Gallant Garden}} * [[Gokoku Shrines]] * {{ill|Tomb of the Unknown Soldier|en|Tomb of the Unknown Soldier|WD=Q1541043|lt=Tomb of the Unknown Soldier}} * {{ill|Arlington National Cemetery|en|Arlington National Cemetery|WD=Q216344|lt=Arlington National Cemetery}} * {{ill|Valhalla|en|Valhalla|WD=Q177860|lt=Valhalla}} (home to the souls of fallen warriors in Scandinavian mythology) * {{ill|Walhalla (memorial)|en|Walhalla (memorial)|WD=Q321240|lt=Walhalla Shrine}} (a hall of fame in Germany honoring "commendable and honorable Germans") * {{ill|Eternal Spring Shrine|en|Eternal Spring Shrine|WD=Q4501089|lt=Eternal Spring Shrine}} * {{ill|The common end of myriad good deeds|en|The common end of myriad good deeds|WD=Q9609095|lt=The common end of myriad good deeds}} * {{ill|Greek hero cult|en|Greek hero cult|WD=Q1878906|lt=Greek hero cult}} == Notes == {{Notelist}} == References == {{Reflist}} == External Links == * [http://www.okinawa-gokoku.jp/ [[co:commons:Category:Okinawaken-Gokoku-jinja]][[ja:沖縄県護国神社]] [[co:commons:Category:Okinawaken-Gokoku-jinja]][[ja:沖縄県護国神社]] [[Category:sn_crud|TESTcommons:Category:Okinawaken-Gokoku-jinja]][[ja:沖縄県護国神社]] {{Infobox religious building| religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | image = Okinawaken Gokoku Shrine haiden.jpg | map_type = Japan | coordinates = | map_size = | map_alt = | map_relief = | map_caption = | location = | festival = | consecration_year = 1936 | type = ''[[Gokoku Shrines|Gokoku shrine]]''
(Formerly ''[[Gokoku Shrines|Shokonsha]]'') | leadership = | website = | architecture_style = | founded_by = | established = | date_destroyed = | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | name = Okinawa Prefecture Gokoku Shrine | image = [[File:Okinawaken Gokoku Shrine haiden.jpg|250px|Okinawa Prefecture Gokoku Shrine Haiden]]
Haiden | coordinates = {{Coord|26|12|11.6|N|127|40|33.6|E|scale:25000_region:JP-47_type:landmark|display=inline,title}} | location = 奥武山町44, Naha City, Okinawa Prefecture, Japan | deity = * Local war dead enshrined at {{ill|Yasukuni Shrine|en|Yasukuni Shrine|WD=Q242803|lt=Yasukuni Shrine|1=Yasukuni Shrine}} * War dead from mainland Japan who perished during the {{ill|Battle of Okinawa|en|Battle of Okinawa|WD=Q192660|lt=Battle of Okinawa|1=Battle of Okinawa}} * Civilian victims and student casualties of the war | established = 1936 (Shōwa 11) | festivals = April 23, October 23 }} '''Okinawa Prefecture Gokoku Shrine''' ({{nihongo|沖縄県護国神社|1=Okinawa Gokoku Shrine|Okinawaken Gokoku Jinja}}) is a {{ill|Shinto shrine|en|Shinto shrine|WD=Q845945|lt=Shinto shrine|1=Shinto shrine}} located within {{ill|Onoyama Park|ja|奥武山公園|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Onoyama Park|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 沖縄県営奥武山公園}}, {{ill|Naha|en|Naha|WD=Q181966|lt=Naha|1=Naha}}, Okinawa Prefecture, Japan. The shrine is an independent religious institution not affiliated with [[:en:Jinja Honcho|Jinja Honcho]]. {{ill|Okinawa Gokoku Shrine|ja|沖縄県護国神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Okinawa Gokoku Shrine|qq=}} is a {{ill|Shinto shrine|en|Shinto shrine|WD=Q845945|lt=Shinto shrine|1=Shinto shrine}} located in {{ill|Japan|en|Japan|WD=Q17|lt=Japan|1=Japan}}. It is a [[Gokoku Shrines|Gokoku Shrine]], or a shrine dedicated to war dead.{{Efn|Is this [[WP:BLUE]] considering that the shrine is literally named as such?}} Such shrines were made to serve to enshrine the war dead, and they were all considered "branches" of {{ill|Yasukuni Shrine|en|Yasukuni Shrine|WD=Q242803|lt=Yasukuni Shrine|1=Yasukuni Shrine}}. They were renamed from {{ill|Shōkonsha|en|Shōkonsha|WD=Q11497810|lt=Shokonsha|1=Shōkonsha}} in 1939.{{Cite journal |last=TAKAYAMA |first=K. PETER |date=1990 |title=Enshrinement and Persistency of Japanese Religion |url=https://www.jstor.org/stable/23917081 |journal=Journal of Church and State |volume=32 |issue=3 |pages=527–547 |issn=0021-969X}} The Okinawa Gokoku-jinja Shrine also enshrines the dead of the {{ill|Battle of Okinawa|en|Battle of Okinawa|WD=Q192660|lt=Battle of Okinawa|1=Battle of Okinawa}}, including ordinary residents, schoolchildren in distress, and civilian war dead.[[護国神社#山中|山中 2013]]、212頁{{Cite web |date=2022-03-08 |title=沖縄県護国神社について｜初詣、ご祈願、地鎮祭は、沖縄県護国神社 |url=https://www.okinawa-gokoku.jp/about.php |url-status=dead |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20220308190702/https://www.okinawa-gokoku.jp/about.php |archive-date=8 March 2022 |access-date=2022-03-08 |website=}} In addition, about 10,000 mobilized students and female volunteer corps members who were victims of the {{ill|Atomic bomb|en|Atomic bomb|WD=Q650051|lt=Atomic bomb|1=Atomic bomb}} are also enshrined as deities at [[Hiroshima Gokoku Shrine]].[[護国神社#山中|山中 2013]]、154頁{{Cite web |date=2021-01-20 |title=由緒 {{!}} 広島護國神社 |url=https://www.h-gokoku.or.jp/yuisho/ |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20210120233005/https://www.h-gokoku.or.jp/yuisho/ |archive-date=2021-01-20 |access-date=2022-03-08}} The main hall burned down during the {{ill|Pacific War|en|Pacific War|WD=Q184425|lt=Pacific War|1=Pacific War}} and was reconstructed in 1959.{{Cite book |last=Takenaka |first=Akiko |url=http://dx.doi.org/10.1515/9780824856939 |title=Yasukuni Shrine |date=2017-10-16 |publisher=University of Hawaii Press |isbn=978-0-8248-5693-9 |pages=158}} == Enshrined Deities == The shrine enshrines spirits of war dead from Okinawa Prefecture and mainland Japan, including civilians and student casualties who perished during the {{ill|Battle of Okinawa|en|Battle of Okinawa|WD=Q192660|lt=Battle of Okinawa|1=Battle of Okinawa}}. As of October 2005, the shrine enshrines 178,689 spirits, the highest number among {{ill|Gokoku shrines|en|Gokoku shrines|WD=Q1534477|lt=Gokoku shrines|1=Gokoku Shrines}} nationwide. == History == [[File:Okinawaken_Gogoku_Shrine_torii.jpeg|thumb|right|170px|Torii gate]] The shrine traces its origins to a {{ill|Shōkonsha|ja|招魂社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shōkonsha|comment=changed title based on wikidata|1=Shōkonsha|WD=Q11497810}} established in 1936 to honor those who died in service to Japan during conflicts such as the {{ill|First Sino-Japanese War|en|First Sino-Japanese War|WD=Q178687|lt=First Sino-Japanese War|1=First Sino-Japanese War}} and the {{ill|Russo-Japanese War|en|Russo-Japanese War|WD=Q159950|lt=Russo-Japanese War|1=Russo-Japanese War}}.加治順人『沖縄の神社』p115　ひるぎ社、2000th year 10月30日 In 1940, it was designated as a Gokoku Shrine by the Ministry of Home Affairs and renamed "Okinawa Prefecture Gokoku Shrine."『全國護國神社會五十年史』（1997th year 3月1日、全國護國神社會発行）287頁。 The shrine was destroyed during the {{ill|Battle of Okinawa|en|Battle of Okinawa|WD=Q192660|lt=Battle of Okinawa|1=Battle of Okinawa}} in April 1945. A temporary shrine was constructed on April 26, 1959. In the same year, relics of spirits from the {{ill|Yasukuni Shrine|en|Yasukuni Shrine|WD=Q242803|lt=Yasukuni Shrine|1=Yasukuni Shrine}} were transferred to the shrine, honoring mainland Japanese who died in the Battle of Okinawa. The current main shrine building was completed in October 1965, with the relocation ceremony held on November 19 and the restoration commemorative festival on November 20.加治順人『沖縄の神社』p143　ひるぎ社、2000th year 10月30日 In 1980, a Kaguraden (dance hall) was added to the grounds. == See Also == * [[Controversies surrounding Yasukuni Shrine]] * {{ill|Martyrs' shrines (China)|en|Martyrs' shrines (China)|WD=Q6800982|lt=Martyr's Shrines|1=Martyrs' shrines (China)}} * {{ill|Martial temple|en|Martial temple|WD=Q11124210|lt=Martial temple|1=Martial temple}} and {{ill|Wen Wu temple|en|Wen Wu temple|WD=Q7231588|lt=Wen Wu temple|1=Wen Wu temple}} * {{ill|National Revolutionary Martyrs' Shrine|en|National Revolutionary Martyrs' Shrine|WD=Q5956488|lt=National Revolutionary Martyrs' It is a [[Gokoku Shrines|Gokoku Shrine]], or a shrine dedicated to war dead.{{Efn|Is this [[WP:BLUE]] considering that the shrine is literally named as such?}} Such shrines were made to serve to enshrine the war dead, and they were all considered "branches" of {{ill|Yasukuni Shrine|en|Yasukuni Shrine|WD=Q242803|lt=Yasukuni Shrine|1=Yasukuni Shrine}}. As of October 2005, the shrine enshrines 178,689 spirits, the highest number among {{ill|Gokoku shrines|en|Gokoku shrines|WD=Q1534477|lt=Gokoku shrines|1=Gokoku Shrines}} nationwide. == History == [[File:Okinawaken_Gogoku_Shrine_torii.jpeg|thumb|right|170px|Torii gate]] The shrine traces its origins to a {{ill|Shōkonsha|ja|招魂社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shōkonsha|comment=changed title based on wikidata|1=Shōkonsha|WD=Q11497810}} established in 1936 to honor those who died in service to Japan during conflicts such as the {{ill|First Sino-Japanese War|en|First Sino-Japanese War|WD=Q178687|lt=First Sino-Japanese War|1=First Sino-Japanese War}} and the {{ill|Russo-Japanese War|en|Russo-Japanese War|WD=Q159950|lt=Russo-Japanese War|1=Russo-Japanese War}}.加治順人『沖縄の神社』p115　ひるぎ社、2000th year 10月30日 In 1940, it was designated as a Gokoku Shrine by the Ministry of Home Affairs and renamed "Okinawa Prefecture Gokoku Shrine."『全國護國神社會五十年史』（1997th year 3月1日、全國護國神社會発行）287頁。 The shrine was destroyed during the {{ill|Battle of Okinawa|en|Battle of Okinawa|WD=Q192660|lt=Battle of Okinawa|1=Battle of Okinawa}} in April 1945. A temporary shrine was constructed on April 26, 1959. In the same year, relics of spirits from the {{ill|Yasukuni Shrine|en|Yasukuni Shrine|WD=Q242803|lt=Yasukuni Shrine|1=Yasukuni Shrine}} were transferred to the shrine, honoring mainland Japanese who died in the Battle of Okinawa. The current main shrine building was completed in October 1965, with the relocation ceremony held on November 19 and the restoration commemorative festival on November 20.加治順人『沖縄の神社』p143　ひるぎ社、2000th [[co:commons:Category:Okinawaken-Gokoku-jinja]][[ja:沖縄県護国神社]] [[co:commons:Category:Okinawaken-Gokoku-jinja]][[ja:沖縄県護国神社]] [[Category:sn_crud|TESTcommons:Category:Okinawaken-Gokoku-jinja]][[ja:沖縄県護国神社]] {{Infobox religious building| religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | image = Okinawaken Gokoku Shrine haiden.jpg | map_type = Japan | coordinates = | map_size = | map_alt = | map_relief = | map_caption = | location = | festival = | consecration_year = 1936 | type = ''[[Gokoku Shrines|Gokoku shrine]]''
(Formerly ''[[Gokoku Shrines|Shokonsha]]'') | leadership = | website = | architecture_style = | founded_by = | established = | date_destroyed = | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | name = Okinawa Prefecture Gokoku Shrine | image = [[File:Okinawaken Gokoku Shrine haiden.jpg|250px|Okinawa Prefecture Gokoku Shrine Haiden]]
Haiden | coordinates = {{Coord|26|12|11.6|N|127|40|33.6|E|scale:25000_region:JP-47_type:landmark|display=inline,title}} | location = 奥武山町44, Naha City, Okinawa Prefecture, Japan | deity = * Local war dead enshrined at {{ill|Yasukuni Shrine|en|Yasukuni Shrine|WD=Q242803|lt=Yasukuni Shrine|1=Yasukuni Shrine}} * War dead from mainland Japan who perished during the {{ill|Battle of Okinawa|en|Battle of Okinawa|WD=Q192660|lt=Battle of Okinawa|1=Battle of Okinawa}} * Civilian victims and student casualties of the war | established = 1936 (Shōwa 11) | festivals = April 23, October 23 }} '''Okinawa Prefecture Gokoku Shrine''' ({{nihongo|沖縄県護国神社|1=Okinawa Gokoku Shrine|Okinawaken Gokoku Jinja}}) is a {{ill|Shinto shrine|en|Shinto shrine|WD=Q845945|lt=Shinto shrine|1=Shinto shrine}} located within {{ill|Onoyama Park|ja|奥武山公園|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Onoyama Park|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 沖縄県営奥武山公園}}, {{ill|Naha|en|Naha|WD=Q181966|lt=Naha|1=Naha}}, Okinawa Prefecture, Japan. The shrine is an independent religious institution not affiliated with [[:en:Jinja Honcho|Jinja Honcho]]. {{ill|Okinawa Gokoku Shrine|ja|沖縄県護国神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Okinawa Gokoku Shrine|qq=}} is a {{ill|Shinto shrine|en|Shinto shrine|WD=Q845945|lt=Shinto shrine|1=Shinto shrine}} located in {{ill|Japan|en|Japan|WD=Q17|lt=Japan|1=Japan}}. It is a [[Gokoku Shrines|Gokoku Shrine]], or a shrine dedicated to war dead.{{Efn|Is this [[WP:BLUE]] considering that the shrine is literally named as such?}} Such shrines were made to serve to enshrine the war dead, and they were all considered "branches" of {{ill|Yasukuni Shrine|en|Yasukuni Shrine|WD=Q242803|lt=Yasukuni Shrine|1=Yasukuni Shrine}}. They were renamed from {{ill|Shōkonsha|en|Shōkonsha|WD=Q11497810|lt=Shokonsha|1=Shōkonsha}} in 1939.{{Cite journal |last=TAKAYAMA |first=K. PETER |date=1990 |title=Enshrinement and Persistency of Japanese Religion |url=https://www.jstor.org/stable/23917081 |journal=Journal of Church and State |volume=32 |issue=3 |pages=527–547 |issn=0021-969X}} The Okinawa Gokoku-jinja Shrine also enshrines the dead of the {{ill|Battle of Okinawa|en|Battle of Okinawa|WD=Q192660|lt=Battle of Okinawa|1=Battle of Okinawa}}, including ordinary residents, schoolchildren in distress, and civilian war dead.[[護国神社#山中|山中 2013]]、212頁{{Cite web |date=2022-03-08 |title=沖縄県護国神社について｜初詣、ご祈願、地鎮祭は、沖縄県護国神社 |url=https://www.okinawa-gokoku.jp/about.php |url-status=dead |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20220308190702/https://www.okinawa-gokoku.jp/about.php |archive-date=8 March 2022 |access-date=2022-03-08 |website=}} In addition, about 10,000 mobilized students and female volunteer corps members who were victims of the {{ill|Atomic bomb|en|Atomic bomb|WD=Q650051|lt=Atomic bomb|1=Atomic bomb}} are also enshrined as deities at [[Hiroshima Gokoku Shrine]].[[護国神社#山中|山中 2013]]、154頁{{Cite web |date=2021-01-20 |title=由緒 {{!}} 広島護國神社 |url=https://www.h-gokoku.or.jp/yuisho/ |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20210120233005/https://www.h-gokoku.or.jp/yuisho/ |archive-date=2021-01-20 |access-date=2022-03-08}} The main hall burned down during the {{ill|Pacific War|en|Pacific War|WD=Q184425|lt=Pacific War|1=Pacific War}} and was reconstructed in 1959.{{Cite book |last=Takenaka |first=Akiko |url=http://dx.doi.org/10.1515/9780824856939 |title=Yasukuni Shrine |date=2017-10-16 |publisher=University of Hawaii Press |isbn=978-0-8248-5693-9 |pages=158}} == Enshrined Deities == The shrine enshrines spirits of war dead from Okinawa Prefecture and mainland Japan, including civilians and student casualties who perished during the {{ill|Battle of Okinawa|en|Battle of Okinawa|WD=Q192660|lt=Battle of Okinawa|1=Battle of Okinawa}}. As of October 2005, the shrine enshrines 178,689 spirits, the highest number among {{ill|Gokoku shrines|en|Gokoku shrines|WD=Q1534477|lt=Gokoku shrines|1=Gokoku Shrines}} nationwide. == History == [[File:Okinawaken_Gogoku_Shrine_torii.jpeg|thumb|right|170px|Torii gate]] The shrine traces its origins to a {{ill|Shōkonsha|ja|招魂社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shōkonsha|comment=changed title based on wikidata|1=Shōkonsha|WD=Q11497810}} established in 1936 to honor those who died in service to Japan during conflicts such as the {{ill|First Sino-Japanese War|en|First Sino-Japanese War|WD=Q178687|lt=First Sino-Japanese War|1=First Sino-Japanese War}} and the {{ill|Russo-Japanese War|en|Russo-Japanese War|WD=Q159950|lt=Russo-Japanese War|1=Russo-Japanese War}}.加治順人『沖縄の神社』p115　ひるぎ社、2000th year 10月30日 In 1940, it was designated as a Gokoku Shrine by the Ministry of Home Affairs and renamed "Okinawa Prefecture Gokoku Shrine."『全國護國神社會五十年史』（1997th year 3月1日、全國護國神社會発行）287頁。 The shrine was destroyed during the {{ill|Battle of Okinawa|en|Battle of Okinawa|WD=Q192660|lt=Battle of Okinawa|1=Battle of Okinawa}} in April 1945. A temporary shrine was constructed on April 26, 1959. In the same year, relics of spirits from the {{ill|Yasukuni Shrine|en|Yasukuni Shrine|WD=Q242803|lt=Yasukuni Shrine|1=Yasukuni Shrine}} were transferred to the shrine, honoring mainland Japanese who died in the Battle of Okinawa. The current main shrine building was completed in October 1965, with the relocation ceremony held on November 19 and the restoration commemorative festival on November 20.加治順人『沖縄の神社』p143　ひるぎ社、2000th year 10月30日 In 1980, a Kaguraden (dance hall) was added to the grounds. == See Also == * [[Controversies surrounding Yasukuni Shrine]] * {{ill|Martyrs' shrines (China)|en|Martyrs' shrines (China)|WD=Q6800982|lt=Martyr's Shrines|1=Martyrs' shrines (China)}} * {{ill|Martial temple|en|Martial temple|WD=Q11124210|lt=Martial temple|1=Martial temple}} and {{ill|Wen Wu temple|en|Wen Wu temple|WD=Q7231588|lt=Wen Wu temple|1=Wen Wu temple}} * {{ill|National Revolutionary Martyrs' Shrine|en|National Revolutionary Martyrs' Shrine|WD=Q5956488|lt=National Revolutionary Martyrs' In the same year, relics of spirits from the {{ill|Yasukuni Shrine|en|Yasukuni Shrine|WD=Q242803|lt=Yasukuni Shrine|1=Yasukuni Shrine}} were transferred to the shrine, honoring mainland Japanese who died in the Battle of Okinawa. It is a [[Gokoku Shrines|Gokoku Shrine]], or a shrine dedicated to war dead.{{Efn|Is this [[WP:BLUE]] considering that the shrine is literally named as such?}} Such shrines were made to serve to enshrine the war dead, and they were all considered "branches" of {{ill|Yasukuni Shrine|en|Yasukuni Shrine|WD=Q242803|lt=Yasukuni Shrine|1=Yasukuni Shrine}}. They were renamed from {{ill|Shōkonsha|en|Shōkonsha|WD=Q11497810|lt=Shokonsha|1=Shōkonsha}} in 1939.{{Cite journal |last=TAKAYAMA |first=K. PETER |date=1990 |title=Enshrinement and Persistency of Japanese Religion |url=https://www.jstor.org/stable/23917081 |journal=Journal of Church and State |volume=32 |issue=3 |pages=527–547 |issn=0021-969X}} The Okinawa Gokoku-jinja Shrine also enshrines the dead of the {{ill|Battle of Okinawa|en|Battle of Okinawa|WD=Q192660|lt=Battle of Okinawa|1=Battle of Okinawa}}, including ordinary residents, schoolchildren in distress, and civilian war dead.[[護国神社#山中|山中 2013]]、212頁{{Cite web |date=2022-03-08 |title=沖縄県護国神社について｜初詣、ご祈願、地鎮祭は、沖縄県護国神社 |url=https://www.okinawa-gokoku.jp/about.php |url-status=dead |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20220308190702/https://www.okinawa-gokoku.jp/about.php |archive-date=8 March 2022 |access-date=2022-03-08 |website=}} In addition, about 10,000 mobilized students and female volunteer corps members who were victims of the {{ill|Atomic bomb|en|Atomic bomb|WD=Q650051|lt=Atomic bomb|1=Atomic bomb}} are also enshrined as deities at [[Hiroshima Gokoku Shrine]].[[護国神社#山中|山中 2013]]、154頁{{Cite web |date=2021-01-20 |title=由緒 {{!}} 広島護國神社 |url=https://www.h-gokoku.or.jp/yuisho/ |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20210120233005/https://www.h-gokoku.or.jp/yuisho/ |archive-date=2021-01-20 |access-date=2022-03-08}} The main hall burned down during the {{ill|Pacific War|en|Pacific War|WD=Q184425|lt=Pacific War|1=Pacific War}} and was reconstructed in 1959.{{Cite book |last=Takenaka |first=Akiko |url=http://dx.doi.org/10.1515/9780824856939 |title=Yasukuni Shrine |date=2017-10-16 |publisher=University of Hawaii Press |isbn=978-0-8248-5693-9 |pages=158}} == Enshrined Deities == The shrine enshrines spirits of war dead from Okinawa Prefecture and mainland Japan, including civilians and student casualties who perished during the {{ill|Battle of Okinawa|en|Battle of Okinawa|WD=Q192660|lt=Battle of Okinawa|1=Battle of Okinawa}}. As of October 2005, the shrine enshrines 178,689 spirits, the highest number among {{ill|Gokoku shrines|en|Gokoku shrines|WD=Q1534477|lt=Gokoku shrines|1=Gokoku Shrines}} nationwide. == History == [[File:Okinawaken_Gogoku_Shrine_torii.jpeg|thumb|right|170px|Torii gate]] The shrine traces its origins to a {{ill|Shōkonsha|ja|招魂社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shōkonsha|comment=changed title based on wikidata|1=Shōkonsha|WD=Q11497810}} established in 1936 to honor those who died in service to Japan during conflicts such as the {{ill|First Sino-Japanese War|en|First Sino-Japanese War|WD=Q178687|lt=First Sino-Japanese War|1=First Sino-Japanese War}} and the {{ill|Russo-Japanese War|en|Russo-Japanese War|WD=Q159950|lt=Russo-Japanese War|1=Russo-Japanese War}}.加治順人『沖縄の神社』p115　ひるぎ社、2000th year 10月30日 In 1940, it was designated as a Gokoku Shrine by the Ministry of Home Affairs and renamed "Okinawa Prefecture Gokoku Shrine."『全國護國神社會五十年史』（1997th year 3月1日、全國護國神社會発行）287頁。 The shrine was destroyed during the {{ill|Battle of Okinawa|en|Battle of Okinawa|WD=Q192660|lt=Battle of Okinawa|1=Battle of Okinawa}} in April 1945. A temporary shrine was constructed on April 26, 1959. In the same year, relics of spirits from the {{ill|Yasukuni Shrine|en|Yasukuni Shrine|WD=Q242803|lt=Yasukuni Shrine|1=Yasukuni Shrine}} were transferred to the shrine, honoring mainland Japanese who died in the Battle of Okinawa. The current main shrine building was completed in October 1965, with the relocation ceremony held on November 19 and the restoration commemorative festival on November 20.加治順人『沖縄の神社』p143　ひるぎ社、2000th year 10月30日 In 1980, a Kaguraden (dance hall) was added to the grounds. == See Also == * [[Controversies surrounding Yasukuni Shrine]] * {{ill|Martyrs' shrines (China)|en|Martyrs' shrines (China)|WD=Q6800982|lt=Martyr's Shrines|1=Martyrs' shrines (China)}} * {{ill|Martial temple|en|Martial temple|WD=Q11124210|lt=Martial temple|1=Martial temple}} and {{ill|Wen Wu temple|en|Wen Wu temple|WD=Q7231588|lt=Wen Wu temple|1=Wen Wu temple}} * {{ill|National Revolutionary Martyrs' Shrine|en|National Revolutionary Martyrs' Shrine|WD=Q5956488|lt=National Revolutionary Martyrs' Shrine|1=National Revolutionary Martyrs' Shrine}} * {{ill|Eternal Spring Shrine|en|Eternal Spring Shrine|WD=Q4501089|lt=Eternal Spring Shrine|1=Eternal Spring Shrine}} * {{ill|Chinese Cultural Renaissance|en|Chinese Cultural Renaissance|WD=Q1073969|lt=Chinese Cultural Renaissance|1=Chinese Cultural Renaissance}} * [[Ancestral shrine]] * {{ill|Gallant Garden|en|Gallant Garden|WD=Q5518867|lt=Gallant Garden|1=Gallant Garden}} * [[Gokoku Shrines]] * {{ill|Tomb of the Unknown Soldier|en|Tomb of the Unknown Soldier|WD=Q1541043|lt=Tomb of the Unknown Soldier|1=Tomb of the Unknown Soldier}} * {{ill|Arlington National Cemetery|en|Arlington National Cemetery|WD=Q216344|lt=Arlington National Cemetery|1=Arlington National Cemetery}} * {{ill|Valhalla|en|Valhalla|WD=Q177860|lt=Valhalla|1=Valhalla}} (home to the souls of fallen warriors in Scandinavian mythology) * {{ill|Walhalla (memorial)|en|Walhalla (memorial)|WD=Q321240|lt=Walhalla Shrine|1=Walhalla (memorial)}} (a hall of fame in Germany honoring "commendable and honorable Germans") * {{ill|Eternal Spring Shrine|en|Eternal Spring Shrine|WD=Q4501089|lt=Eternal Spring Shrine|1=Eternal Spring Shrine}} * {{ill|The common end of myriad good deeds|en|The common end of myriad good deeds|WD=Q9609095|lt=The common end of myriad good deeds|1=The common end of myriad good deeds}} * {{ill|Greek hero cult|en|Greek hero cult|WD=Q1878906|lt=Greek hero cult|1=Greek hero cult}} == Notes == {{Notelist}} == References == {{Reflist}} == They were renamed from {{ill|Shōkonsha|en|Shōkonsha|WD=Q11497810|lt=Shokonsha|1=Shōkonsha}} in 1939.{{Cite journal |last=TAKAYAMA |first=K. As of October 2005, the shrine enshrines 178,689 spirits, the highest number among {{ill|Gokoku shrines|en|Gokoku shrines|WD=Q1534477|lt=Gokoku shrines|1=Gokoku Shrines}} nationwide. == History == [[File:Okinawaken_Gogoku_Shrine_torii.jpeg|thumb|right|170px|Torii gate]] The shrine traces its origins to a {{ill|Shōkonsha|ja|招魂社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shōkonsha|comment=changed title based on wikidata|1=Shōkonsha|WD=Q11497810}} established in 1936 to honor those who died in service to Japan during conflicts such as the {{ill|First Sino-Japanese War|en|First Sino-Japanese War|WD=Q178687|lt=First Sino-Japanese War|1=First Sino-Japanese War}} and the {{ill|Russo-Japanese War|en|Russo-Japanese War|WD=Q159950|lt=Russo-Japanese War|1=Russo-Japanese War}}.加治順人『沖縄の神社』p115　ひるぎ社、2000th year 10月30日 In 1940, it was designated as a Gokoku Shrine by the Ministry of Home Affairs and renamed "Okinawa Prefecture Gokoku Shrine."『全國護國神社會五十年史』（1997th year 3月1日、全國護國神社會発行）287頁。 The shrine was destroyed during the {{ill|Battle of Okinawa|en|Battle of Okinawa|WD=Q192660|lt=Battle of Okinawa|1=Battle of Okinawa}} in April 1945. A temporary shrine was constructed on April 26, 1959. In the same year, relics of spirits from the {{ill|Yasukuni Shrine|en|Yasukuni Shrine|WD=Q242803|lt=Yasukuni Shrine|1=Yasukuni Shrine}} were transferred to the shrine, honoring mainland Japanese who died in the Battle of Okinawa. The current main shrine building was completed in October 1965, with the relocation ceremony held on November 19 and the restoration commemorative festival on November 20.加治順人『沖縄の神社』p143　ひるぎ社、2000th year 10月30日 In 1980, a Kaguraden (dance hall) was added to the grounds. == See Also == * [[Controversies surrounding Yasukuni Shrine]] * {{ill|Martyrs' shrines (China)|en|Martyrs' shrines (China)|WD=Q6800982|lt=Martyr's Shrines|1=Martyrs' shrines (China)}} * {{ill|Martial temple|en|Martial temple|WD=Q11124210|lt=Martial temple|1=Martial temple}} and {{ill|Wen Wu temple|en|Wen Wu temple|WD=Q7231588|lt=Wen Wu temple|1=Wen Wu temple}} * {{ill|National Revolutionary Martyrs' Shrine|en|National Revolutionary Martyrs' Shrine|WD=Q5956488|lt=National Revolutionary Martyrs' Shrine|1=National Revolutionary Martyrs' Shrine}} * {{ill|Eternal Spring Shrine|en|Eternal Spring Shrine|WD=Q4501089|lt=Eternal Spring Shrine|1=Eternal Spring Shrine}} * {{ill|Chinese Cultural Renaissance|en|Chinese Cultural Renaissance|WD=Q1073969|lt=Chinese Cultural Renaissance|1=Chinese Cultural Renaissance}} * [[Ancestral shrine]] * {{ill|Gallant Garden|en|Gallant Garden|WD=Q5518867|lt=Gallant Garden|1=Gallant Garden}} * [[Gokoku Shrines]] * {{ill|Tomb of the Unknown Soldier|en|Tomb of the Unknown Soldier|WD=Q1541043|lt=Tomb of the Unknown Soldier|1=Tomb of the Unknown Soldier}} * {{ill|Arlington National Cemetery|en|Arlington National Cemetery|WD=Q216344|lt=Arlington National Cemetery|1=Arlington National Cemetery}} * {{ill|Valhalla|en|Valhalla|WD=Q177860|lt=Valhalla|1=Valhalla}} (home to the souls of fallen warriors in Scandinavian mythology) * {{ill|Walhalla (memorial)|en|Walhalla (memorial)|WD=Q321240|lt=Walhalla Shrine|1=Walhalla (memorial)}} (a hall of fame in Germany honoring "commendable and honorable Germans") * {{ill|Eternal Spring Shrine|en|Eternal Spring Shrine|WD=Q4501089|lt=Eternal Spring Shrine|1=Eternal Spring Shrine}} * {{ill|The common end of myriad good deeds|en|The common end of myriad good deeds|WD=Q9609095|lt=The common end of myriad good deeds|1=The common end of myriad good deeds}} * {{ill|Greek hero cult|en|Greek hero cult|WD=Q1878906|lt=Greek hero cult|1=Greek hero cult}} == Notes == {{Notelist}} == References == {{Reflist}} == (Formerly ''[[Gokoku Shrines|Shokonsha]]'') | leadership = | website = | architecture_style = | founded_by = | established = | date_destroyed = | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | name = Okinawa Prefecture Gokoku Shrine | image = [[File:Okinawaken Gokoku Shrine haiden.jpg|250px|Okinawa Prefecture Gokoku Shrine Haiden]]
Haiden | coordinates = {{Coord|26|12|11.6|N|127|40|33.6|E|scale:25000_region:JP-47_type:landmark|display=inline,title}} | location = 奥武山町44, Naha City, Okinawa Prefecture, Japan | deity = * Local war dead enshrined at {{ill|Yasukuni Shrine|en|Yasukuni Shrine|WD=Q242803|lt=Yasukuni Shrine|1=Yasukuni Shrine}} * War dead from mainland Japan who perished during the {{ill|Battle of Okinawa|en|Battle of Okinawa|WD=Q192660|lt=Battle of Okinawa|1=Battle of Okinawa}} * Civilian victims and student casualties of the war | established = 1936 (Shōwa 11) | festivals = April 23, October 23 }} '''Okinawa Prefecture Gokoku Shrine''' ({{nihongo|沖縄県護国神社|1=Okinawa Gokoku Shrine|Okinawaken Gokoku Jinja}}) is a {{ill|Shinto shrine|en|Shinto shrine|WD=Q845945|lt=Shinto shrine|1=Shinto shrine}} located within {{ill|Onoyama Park|ja|奥武山公園|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Onoyama Park|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 沖縄県営奥武山公園}}, {{ill|Naha|en|Naha|WD=Q181966|lt=Naha|1=Naha}}, Okinawa Prefecture, Japan. The shrine is an independent religious institution not affiliated with [[:en:Jinja Honcho|Jinja Honcho]]. {{ill|Okinawa Gokoku Shrine|ja|沖縄県護国神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Okinawa Gokoku Shrine|qq=}} is a {{ill|Shinto shrine|en|Shinto shrine|WD=Q845945|lt=Shinto shrine|1=Shinto shrine}} located in {{ill|Japan|en|Japan|WD=Q17|lt=Japan|1=Japan}}. It is a [[Gokoku Shrines|Gokoku Shrine]], or a shrine dedicated to war dead.{{Efn|Is this [[WP:BLUE]] considering that the shrine is literally named as such?}} Such shrines were made to serve to enshrine the war dead, and they were all considered "branches" of {{ill|Yasukuni Shrine|en|Yasukuni Shrine|WD=Q242803|lt=Yasukuni Shrine|1=Yasukuni Shrine}}. They were renamed from {{ill|Shōkonsha|en|Shōkonsha|WD=Q11497810|lt=Shokonsha|1=Shōkonsha}} in 1939.{{Cite journal |last=TAKAYAMA |first=K. PETER |date=1990 |title=Enshrinement and Persistency of Japanese Religion |url=https://www.jstor.org/stable/23917081 |journal=Journal of Church and State |volume=32 |issue=3 |pages=527–547 |issn=0021-969X}} The Okinawa Gokoku-jinja Shrine also enshrines the dead of the {{ill|Battle of Okinawa|en|Battle of Okinawa|WD=Q192660|lt=Battle of Okinawa|1=Battle of Okinawa}}, including ordinary residents, schoolchildren in distress, and civilian war dead.[[護国神社#山中|山中 2013]]、212頁{{Cite web |date=2022-03-08 |title=沖縄県護国神社について｜初詣、ご祈願、地鎮祭は、沖縄県護国神社 |url=https://www.okinawa-gokoku.jp/about.php |url-status=dead |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20220308190702/https://www.okinawa-gokoku.jp/about.php |archive-date=8 March 2022 |access-date=2022-03-08 |website=}} In addition, about 10,000 mobilized students and female volunteer corps members who were victims of the {{ill|Atomic bomb|en|Atomic bomb|WD=Q650051|lt=Atomic bomb|1=Atomic bomb}} are also enshrined as deities at [[Hiroshima Gokoku Shrine]].[[護国神社#山中|山中 2013]]、154頁{{Cite web |date=2021-01-20 |title=由緒 {{!}} 広島護國神社 |url=https://www.h-gokoku.or.jp/yuisho/ |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20210120233005/https://www.h-gokoku.or.jp/yuisho/ |archive-date=2021-01-20 |access-date=2022-03-08}} The main hall burned down during the {{ill|Pacific War|en|Pacific War|WD=Q184425|lt=Pacific War|1=Pacific War}} and was reconstructed in 1959.{{Cite book |last=Takenaka |first=Akiko |url=http://dx.doi.org/10.1515/9780824856939 |title=Yasukuni Shrine |date=2017-10-16 |publisher=University of Hawaii Press |isbn=978-0-8248-5693-9 |pages=158}} == Enshrined Deities == The shrine enshrines spirits of war dead from Okinawa Prefecture and mainland Japan, including civilians and student casualties who perished during the {{ill|Battle of Okinawa|en|Battle of Okinawa|WD=Q192660|lt=Battle of Okinawa|1=Battle of Okinawa}}. As of October 2005, the shrine enshrines 178,689 spirits, the highest number among {{ill|Gokoku shrines|en|Gokoku shrines|WD=Q1534477|lt=Gokoku shrines|1=Gokoku Shrines}} nationwide. == History == [[File:Okinawaken_Gogoku_Shrine_torii.jpeg|thumb|right|170px|Torii gate]] The shrine traces its origins to a {{ill|Shōkonsha|ja|招魂社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shōkonsha|comment=changed title based on wikidata|1=Shōkonsha|WD=Q11497810}} established in 1936 to honor those who died in service to Japan during conflicts such as the {{ill|First Sino-Japanese War|en|First Sino-Japanese War|WD=Q178687|lt=First Sino-Japanese War|1=First Sino-Japanese War}} and the {{ill|Russo-Japanese War|en|Russo-Japanese War|WD=Q159950|lt=Russo-Japanese War|1=Russo-Japanese War}}.加治順人『沖縄の神社』p115　ひるぎ社、2000th year 10月30日 In 1940, it was designated as a Gokoku Shrine by the Ministry of Home Affairs and renamed "Okinawa Prefecture Gokoku Shrine."『全國護國神社會五十年史』（1997th year 3月1日、全國護國神社會発行）287頁。 The shrine was destroyed during the {{ill|Battle of Okinawa|en|Battle of Okinawa|WD=Q192660|lt=Battle of Okinawa|1=Battle of Okinawa}} in April 1945. A temporary shrine was constructed on April 26, 1959. In the same year, relics of spirits from the {{ill|Yasukuni Shrine|en|Yasukuni Shrine|WD=Q242803|lt=Yasukuni Shrine|1=Yasukuni Shrine}} were transferred to the shrine, honoring mainland Japanese who died in the Battle of Okinawa. The current main shrine building was completed in October 1965, with the relocation ceremony held on November 19 and the restoration commemorative festival on November 20.加治順人『沖縄の神社』p143　ひるぎ社、2000th They were renamed from {{ill|Shōkonsha|en|Shōkonsha|WD=Q11497810|lt=Shokonsha|1=Shōkonsha}} in 1939.{{Cite journal |last=TAKAYAMA |first=K. PETER |date=1990 |title=Enshrinement and Persistency of Japanese Religion |url=https://www.jstor.org/stable/23917081 |journal=Journal of Church and State |volume=32 |issue=3 |pages=527–547 |issn=0021-969X}} The Okinawa Gokoku-jinja Shrine also enshrines the dead of the {{ill|Battle of Okinawa|en|Battle of Okinawa|WD=Q192660|lt=Battle of Okinawa|1=Battle of Okinawa}}, including ordinary residents, schoolchildren in distress, and civilian war dead.[[護国神社#山中|山中 2013]]、212頁{{Cite web |date=2022-03-08 |title=沖縄県護国神社について｜初詣、ご祈願、地鎮祭は、沖縄県護国神社 |url=https://www.okinawa-gokoku.jp/about.php |url-status=dead |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20220308190702/https://www.okinawa-gokoku.jp/about.php |archive-date=8 March 2022 |access-date=2022-03-08 |website=}} In addition, about 10,000 mobilized students and female volunteer corps members who were victims of the {{ill|Atomic bomb|en|Atomic bomb|WD=Q650051|lt=Atomic bomb|1=Atomic bomb}} are also enshrined as deities at [[Hiroshima Gokoku Shrine]].[[護国神社#山中|山中 2013]]、154頁{{Cite web |date=2021-01-20 |title=由緒 {{!}} 広島護國神社 |url=https://www.h-gokoku.or.jp/yuisho/ |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20210120233005/https://www.h-gokoku.or.jp/yuisho/ |archive-date=2021-01-20 |access-date=2022-03-08}} The main hall burned down during the {{ill|Pacific War|en|Pacific War|WD=Q184425|lt=Pacific War|1=Pacific War}} and was reconstructed in 1959.{{Cite book |last=Takenaka |first=Akiko |url=http://dx.doi.org/10.1515/9780824856939 |title=Yasukuni Shrine |date=2017-10-16 |publisher=University of Hawaii Press |isbn=978-0-8248-5693-9 |pages=158}} == Enshrined Deities == The shrine enshrines spirits of war dead from Okinawa Prefecture and mainland Japan, including civilians and student casualties who perished during the {{ill|Battle of Okinawa|en|Battle of Okinawa|WD=Q192660|lt=Battle of Okinawa|1=Battle of Okinawa}}. As of October 2005, the shrine enshrines 178,689 spirits, the highest number among {{ill|Gokoku shrines|en|Gokoku shrines|WD=Q1534477|lt=Gokoku shrines|1=Gokoku Shrines}} nationwide. == History == [[File:Okinawaken_Gogoku_Shrine_torii.jpeg|thumb|right|170px|Torii gate]] The shrine traces its origins to a {{ill|Shōkonsha|ja|招魂社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shōkonsha|comment=changed title based on wikidata|1=Shōkonsha|WD=Q11497810}} established in 1936 to honor those who died in service to Japan during conflicts such as the {{ill|First Sino-Japanese War|en|First Sino-Japanese War|WD=Q178687|lt=First Sino-Japanese War|1=First Sino-Japanese War}} and the {{ill|Russo-Japanese War|en|Russo-Japanese War|WD=Q159950|lt=Russo-Japanese War|1=Russo-Japanese War}}.加治順人『沖縄の神社』p115　ひるぎ社、2000th year 10月30日 In 1940, it was designated as a Gokoku Shrine by the Ministry of Home Affairs and renamed "Okinawa Prefecture Gokoku Shrine."『全國護國神社會五十年史』（1997th year 3月1日、全國護國神社會発行）287頁。 The shrine was destroyed during the {{ill|Battle of Okinawa|en|Battle of Okinawa|WD=Q192660|lt=Battle of Okinawa|1=Battle of Okinawa}} in April 1945. A temporary shrine was constructed on April 26, 1959. In the same year, relics of spirits from the {{ill|Yasukuni Shrine|en|Yasukuni Shrine|WD=Q242803|lt=Yasukuni Shrine|1=Yasukuni Shrine}} were transferred to the shrine, honoring mainland Japanese who died in the Battle of Okinawa. The current main shrine building was completed in October 1965, with the relocation ceremony held on November 19 and the restoration commemorative festival on November 20.加治順人『沖縄の神社』p143　ひるぎ社、2000th year 10月30日 In 1980, a Kaguraden (dance hall) was added to the grounds. == See Also == * [[Controversies surrounding Yasukuni Shrine]] * {{ill|Martyrs' shrines (China)|en|Martyrs' shrines (China)|WD=Q6800982|lt=Martyr's Shrines|1=Martyrs' shrines (China)}} * {{ill|Martial temple|en|Martial temple|WD=Q11124210|lt=Martial temple|1=Martial temple}} and {{ill|Wen Wu temple|en|Wen Wu
